directorates.title: Adults, Health & Wellbeing

savings_delivery_history.note: Catastrophic: £31M shortfall. ASC alone caused £21.6M overspend
Half delivered. Demand growth outstripped savings

directorates.savings_narrative: Adult Social Care is the council's largest budget (59.3% of net revenue) and the single biggest reform opportunity. The CQC 'Requires Improvement' rating exposes operational failures that directly drive excess costs: 31% of home care commissioned off-framework at 20% premium (£3-5M recoverable), CHC recovery running at half the national rate (£5-12M from NHS ICB), and a residential-heavy model (£172M/yr vs £57M/yr home care) that costs 3x what community care would. The 2024/25 savings target of £34.8M delivered only £3.8M, a £31M shortfall that proves the previous administration's approach failed. Public Health adds long-term ROI: every £1 in falls prevention saves £3.50 in ASC, but the HCRG near-monopoly (85% of category, HHI 3,615) and zero Contracts Finder visibility suggest procurement reform could yield £2-5M.

directorates.title: Education & Children's Services
directorates.savings_narrative: Children's Services has an Ofsted 'Good' rating but faces a SEND crisis (DfE Improvement Notice, DSG deficit spiralling from £22M to £545M) and a residential placement cost spiral (agency homes £6K/week, up £400 in 18 months). The biggest savings come from SEND transport reform (£5-10M through fleet expansion, route optimisation, Personal Travel Budgets), residential step-down (each child moved from £312K/yr residential to £30K/yr fostering = £282K saving), and the WOCL programme (30 in-house homes saving £2.1M/yr vs agency). The agency workforce crisis (110 agency EPs vs 11 permanent, 10:1 ratio) is an immediate cost driver. Lancashire has 8% of all children's homes in England (347 total), creating a perverse market where the council pays premium rates for placements in its own county.

directorates.title: Growth, Environment & Transport
directorates.savings_narrative: This directorate combines highways (the most visible public service), waste (the most locked-in contracts), and economic development (the most discretionary). The managed highway service model, just 6 months old, has already delivered 42% fewer defects at 50% lower cost per m-sq, with the first UK trial of asphalt recycling. Extending this model is the easiest win. Waste is the hardest challenge: Lancashire Renewables (£71M, wholly-owned) + Suez (£46M, 60.7% of category) = £117M with an expired waste strategy (2020) and no EfW decision. The energy contracts (£170.8M combined, both flagged weak competition) are a cross-cutting opportunity. S59 utility recharges and street lighting are low-risk income/efficiency plays. Economic development savings come from programme rationalisation and CCA leverage.

directorates.title: Resources & Digital
directorates.savings_narrative: Resources & Digital is the reform engine room. Net budget is small (£42M) but centralised savings potential is massive: £93.6M in potential duplicates (most likely Oracle artifacts but must be confirmed), 3-5% on £400M+ addressable procurement spend (£5-15M), £3-8M from property rationalisation (1,200+ assets), and the Oracle transparency fix (100% empty descriptions) which is the single biggest enabler for all other savings. The DSG deficit time bomb (£22M→£545M by 2028) and reserves trajectory (£420M→£223M in 4 years) create existential risk. Reserves at 2.25% of general fund is dangerously close to S114 territory. Digital transformation (channel shift, AI automation, system consolidation) offers £4-11M. The Softcat £64M IT contract and Netcall £4.3M AI/CRM are recent investments that should now deliver returns.

evidence.benchmark: Comparable county councils (Surrey, Kent, Hampshire) have similar-sized senior teams but fewer interims. Interim premium typically 30-50% above permanent
evidence.calculation: 4 interims × £30K premium = £120K/yr on interims alone. Full restructuring of 19 posts over £100K: if 5 eliminated or reduced = £500K-£1M. Wider corporate centre review: £2-5M
evidence.kpi_link: Organisational stability is implicit in all inspection frameworks. Multiple interims signal instability to CQC, Ofsted, and DfE

Convert or eliminate 4 interim positions as permanents are recruited
Benchmark senior team size against comparable county councils
Implement restructuring using redundancy provision

evidence.political_framing: Nineteen staff on over £100,000 and four interim directors at premium rates. We're stabilising the top of the organisation and making sure every senior post delivers value.

data_points: LCC contributes to multiple partnership bodies (LEP, CCA, LGA, district partnerships)

evidence.benchmark: Other county councils reviewing partnership costs as part of MTFS. Kent saved £400K from partnership audit 2024
evidence.calculation: Estimated 8-12 partnership memberships × £50-200K average = £400K-2.4M total. 20-40% reduction through audit = £80-960K. Conservative range: £0.5-1M
evidence.kpi_link: No direct KPI link. This is pure efficiency review

implementation_steps.step: Compile full register of partnership memberships, subscriptions and secondments
Assess value/benefit of each: what does LCC get in return?
Negotiate reduced contributions or exit low-value partnerships

evidence.political_framing: We need to know exactly what every partnership subscription costs and what it delivers. If it doesn't deliver value for Lancashire taxpayers, we stop paying.

savings_levers.description: Freeze all member allowances (basic, SRA, travel, subsistence) at current levels for two financial years. Saving is modest but the political signal is powerful: elected members share the burden of savings before asking officers and staff to accept cuts.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Local Authorities (Members' Allowances) (England) Regulations 2003, Independent Remuneration Panel recommendation (can be overridden by Full Council)

evidence.benchmark: Multiple councils froze allowances during austerity (2010-2015). Reform messaging: lead from the front.
evidence.calculation: £1.5-2M total allowances. 2-year freeze saves 2 x 2.5% inflation = 5% = £75-100K/yr. Two years: £0.15-0.2M. Small but symbolic.
evidence.kpi_link: Full Council decision, IRP report

implementation_steps.step: Table motion at next Full Council to freeze allowances for 2 years
Accept IRP recommendation or vote to override with freeze
Communicate decision publicly as shared sacrifice

evidence.political_framing: Before we ask a single member of staff to accept change, elected members freeze their own pay. Reform leads from the front.

savings_levers.description: The Combined County Authority devolution deal brings government funding for transport, skills, and housing. Front-loading the drawdown of available grants and negotiating expanded scope for the deal brings forward income that would otherwise arrive in later years. LCC as the largest CCA member should maximise its share.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Levelling Up and Regeneration Act 2023, CCA constitution, MHCLG devolution framework

evidence.benchmark: Greater Manchester CA front-loaded transport investment saving £3M in year 1. West Midlands CA negotiated expanded gainshare.
evidence.calculation: If CCA total funding £200M over 5 years. Front-loading 10% of drawdown = £20M brought forward. LCC share (60%) = £12M. Yield at current rates: £2-5M benefit.
evidence.kpi_link: CCA performance framework, MHCLG devolution monitoring

implementation_steps.step: Map all CCA funding streams and drawdown schedules
Negotiate accelerated drawdown with MHCLG for transport/skills
Maximise LCC share of gainshare and investment fund
Deploy front-loaded funding on high-return projects

evidence.political_framing: Devolution means Lancashire keeps more of its money. We are negotiating hard with Whitehall to get the best deal for Lancashire taxpayers.

savings_levers.description: LCC should establish a central events and conferencing function, commercially marketing County Hall, Woodlands Conference Centre, and other LCC venues for weddings, conferences, corporate events, and civic functions. Currently underused outside office hours and weekends. Quick wins: weekend wedding venue hire at County Hall (Grade II listed, impressive setting), corporate conference packages with catering concessions, room hire rate card at market prices not subsidised rates.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: LGA 2003 s93 (charging), Localism Act 2011 (general power of competence), Licensing Act 2003 (premises licence for alcohol/entertainment), planning (change of use not required for occasional events), health and safety regulations

data_points: County Hall is a Grade II listed building with impressive rooms
Currently minimal commercial hire income from LCC venues
Wedding venue market in Lancashire: GBP 3-8K per weekend booking
Conference market: GBP 500-2K per day for quality venues
evidence.benchmark: Hertfordshire CC generates GBP 0.3M from venue hire. Devon CC: GBP 0.2M. Cheshire East: GBP 0.4M. Many county halls now commercially marketed as wedding and events venues.
evidence.calculation: Weekend weddings (30 at GBP 3-5K): GBP 90-150K. Conferences (100 days at GBP 500-1K): GBP 50-100K. Corporate events (50 at GBP 500-1K): GBP 25-50K. Room hire uplift across all venues: GBP 50-100K. Total: GBP 0.2-0.5M.
evidence.kpi_link: Venue utilisation rate, income per venue, customer satisfaction

implementation_steps.step: Audit all LCC venues for commercial hire potential
Obtain premises licence for County Hall and key venues
Publish venue hire rate card at market rates (not subsidised)
List on wedding and conference booking platforms (Hitched, Venue Scanner)
Appoint 0.5 FTE venue coordinator (self-funding from income)

evidence.political_framing: We have beautiful buildings sitting empty at weekends. Every wedding at County Hall pays for council services. This is common sense, not controversial.

data_points: 347 children's homes in Lancashire (8% of England)
evidence.benchmark: In-house residential typically 30-40% cheaper than agency. Best councils achieve 60%+ in-house placement
evidence.calculation: Full programme: 100 beds × 52 weeks × (£6,000 agency - £4,000 in-house) = £10.4M gross saving. Net of setup/running costs = £2-4M/yr. Each additional child in-house = ~£100K/yr saving

Phase 2: open remaining 11 homes to reach 30 total

evidence.political_framing: We're bringing our children home. Instead of paying private companies £6,000 a week, we're building our own homes: better care, closer to families, and saving the taxpayer £100,000 per child per year.

savings_levers.description: 97 apprentices currently on social work degree programme (UCLan). 20 NQSWs started Jan 2025. Agency rates 40-60% premium. Grow-your-own strategy via Social Work Academy.

data_points: ASC recruited 53 SWs, 14 OTs, 18 SCOs saving £1.6M since April
110 agency EPs in SEND alone (10:1 ratio)
evidence.benchmark: Leading councils achieve <15% agency workforce. National avg ~20%. Lancashire significantly above
evidence.calculation: If agency premium is 50% on £40K base = £20K extra per SW. Converting 50 agency SWs = £1M/yr. Including EPs (110 agency × £30K premium) = £3.3M. Total £3-5M
evidence.kpi_link: Ofsted and CQC both assess workforce stability. High agency reliance = poor continuity for children/adults = worse inspection outcomes. Conversion improves quality AND cost

implementation_steps.step: Accelerate UCLan apprenticeship programme, target 50 graduates/year
Offer agency workers permanent contracts with retention bonuses
Competitive salary review vs agency rates to close the gap
International recruitment campaign for qualified SWs and EPs

evidence.political_framing: We're growing our own workforce instead of paying agencies a 50% premium. 97 apprentices are training right now. That's investment in Lancashire people AND savings for Lancashire taxpayers.

savings_levers.description: Family Safeguarding model already reduced children in care under FS from 388 to 286. For every child stepped down from residential (£312K/yr) to fostering (£30K/yr) = £282K saving. Target 25-40 step-downs.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Care Planning Regulations 2010: must demonstrate placement meets child's needs

evidence.benchmark: National: residential costs £3.1B. Placements >£10K/week up from 120 to 1,510. Step-down programmes at best councils achieve 15-20% annual reduction
evidence.calculation: 25 step-downs × £282K saving per child = £7.05M. 40 step-downs = £11.3M. Conservative £5-10M allowing for transition costs and partial-year effects
evidence.kpi_link: Ofsted judges sufficiency and matching. Better matching through step-down improves stability metrics AND saves money. Family Safeguarding model is the evidence base

implementation_steps.step: Review all 262 residential placements and identify step-down candidates (target 50 reviews)
Match candidates to foster carers (in-house priority, then IFA)
Therapeutic support for transition (stepping stones packages)
Step-down 25 children in year 1, 15 additional in year 2
Track placement stability. If step-down fails, learn and adjust

evidence.political_framing: Every child who moves from a children's home to a loving foster family saves £282,000 a year. That's better for the child AND better for the taxpayer. We're targeting 25-40 step-downs.

savings_levers.description: Foster carer payments £399-523/week + £1,145.50 approval. Refer-a-friend £1,650. In-house costs 60% less than IFA. Target 50 additional carers.

data_points: Foster With Us regional campaign: 760 carers needed across NW, 152 children waiting
evidence.benchmark: Best councils achieve 70%+ in-house fostering. IFA should be exception not norm
evidence.calculation: 50 additional in-house carers × avg 1.5 children each = 75 children. Saving per child: £500/week (IFA £1,000 - in-house £500). 75 × £500 × 52 = £1.95M. At 100 carers = £3.9M
evidence.kpi_link: Ofsted judges sufficiency of foster placements. More in-house carers = better local matching = better stability outcomes AND lower cost

implementation_steps.step: Intensify Foster With Us regional recruitment: targeted social media, community events
Speed up approval process (target 4 months, currently 6-8)
Increase refer-a-friend payment and retention bonuses for experienced carers
Specialist recruitment for UASC, complex needs, sibling groups

evidence.political_framing: Every foster carer we recruit in Lancashire is a family that keeps a child local and saves the taxpayer £25,000 a year compared to agency placements.

savings_levers.description: Intensive family support to prevent children entering care. Family Safeguarding model shows proven results. Every child diverted from care = £40-70K annual saving.

data_points: Family Safeguarding reduced children under FS from 388→286 (-26%)
Each child in care costs £40-70K/yr (fostering) or £312K/yr (residential)
evidence.benchmark: Hertfordshire model (originator of Family Safeguarding): 34% reduction in care entries. National average 4% increase
evidence.calculation: 100 children diverted from care × avg £55K/yr = £5.5M avoided. Family Safeguarding cost per family ~£15K. 100 families × £15K = £1.5M investment. Net £3-6M
evidence.kpi_link: Ofsted rates early help effectiveness. Proven prevention reduces care numbers which reduces placement pressure and agency costs

implementation_steps.step: Expand Family Safeguarding teams to cover all 12 districts
Deploy edge-of-care intensive support workers (target 20 new posts)
Embed adult workers (substance misuse, MH, DV) in children's teams
Track cohort outcomes: children diverted vs entered care

evidence.political_framing: For every family we keep together safely, we save the taxpayer £55,000 a year in care costs. Family Safeguarding works. It's already cut our numbers by 26%.

savings_levers.description: Lancashire has 347 children's homes (8% of England). 330 are private, only 17 LCC-owned. Fylde: 24→52, Wyre: 23→44 homes. Only 149 Lancashire children placed in Lancashire agency homes (15% of 980 capacity). The rest are out-of-area children at premium rates. Use planning, licensing and sufficiency powers to regulate the market.

data_points: 347 children's homes in Lancashire (8% of England total)
Only 149 Lancashire children in Lancashire agency homes (15% of capacity)
Average agency cost £6,000/week, complex £9,000, national extreme £60,000
evidence.benchmark: CMA investigation into children's social care market found 'excess profits' in private provision. ADCS calling for national regulation
evidence.calculation: If 50 Lancashire children currently placed out-of-county (at £7K/week premium over local) are placed in-county: 50 × £1K/week saving × 52 = £2.6M. Market shaping to reduce prices: additional £1-3M
evidence.kpi_link: Ofsted sufficiency assessment considers local placement availability. More local placements = better outcomes (closer to family/school) AND lower cost

implementation_steps.step: Map all 347 homes: capacity, occupancy, placing authority, rates
Engage with district planning authorities on Article 4 directions for children's homes
Negotiate bulk-buy agreements with local providers for Lancashire children
Support DfE/ADCS campaign for national rate regulation

evidence.political_framing: Lancashire has more children's homes than almost anywhere in England, 347 of them. But 85% of the children in those homes aren't from Lancashire. Private companies are making excess profits while our children are sent miles away from their families.

savings_levers.legal_constraints: Limited direct regulatory powers. Ofsted regulates. Planning powers through districts. Market shaping through commissioning

savings_levers.description: Systematic review of Home Office funding claims for 180+ UASC + 58 care leavers. NTS rates often below actual cost. National expenditure £323M (+32% in 2 years).

data_points: Home Office grants below actual cost
evidence.benchmark: National Transfer Scheme rates: £143/day (under 16), £200/day (16-17). Actual costs often £200-400/day
evidence.calculation: 180 UASC × £100/day shortfall × 365 = £6.57M. Improved claiming could recover £2-4M of this gap through accurate cost recording and escalation
evidence.kpi_link: UASC numbers affect overall children in care figures and placement sufficiency (Ofsted metric). Better funding = less pressure on general fund = more capacity for local children

implementation_steps.step: Audit all UASC placement costs vs Home Office grant rates received
Submit revised cost claims for all under-funded placements
Escalate systematic underfunding to DfE/Home Office via LGA/ADCS
Ensure all 1,000+ out-of-area health assessments are charged to placing authority

evidence.political_framing: The Home Office is not paying the full cost of looking after unaccompanied children. Lancashire taxpayers are picking up the tab. We will recover every penny we're owed.

savings_levers.description: Review kinship care allowance rates against fostering equivalents. Some kinship carers receive enhanced rates above the standard fostering allowance. Aligning to a consistent rate structure (matching DfE national minimum fostering allowance) reduces overpayments without reducing support below legal minimums.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Children Act 1989 s22C, DfE kinship care guidance 2024, no legal duty to match foster rates for non-looked-after kinship

data_points: Some rates exceed fostering allowance by 10-20%
DfE national minimum fostering allowance is the floor
evidence.benchmark: Several councils have aligned kinship and fostering rates saving 5-15% on kinship spend. Suffolk CC saved £0.8M from rate alignment.
evidence.calculation: 400 kinship placements x average £15K/yr. If 10% overpayment = £600K. Plus administrative simplification: £0.3-1M.
evidence.kpi_link: Ofsted inspection, children in care stability indicators

implementation_steps.step: Audit all kinship allowance rates against DfE minimums and foster equivalents
Design aligned rate structure with appropriate transition protection
Consult with kinship carers and implement from next financial quarter

evidence.political_framing: We support kinship carers fully, but we need a fair and consistent system. Every family gets the right support at the right rate.

savings_levers.description: LCC pays interagency adoption fees of up to £33K per placement when using agencies outside the regional adoption agency (RAA). Maximising placements through the RAA and reducing interagency fees to £20K average saves £0.5-1.5M across 40-60 annual placements.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Adoption and Children Act 2002, DfE RAA programme requirements

data_points: LCC is part of Adoption Lancashire & Blackpool
evidence.benchmark: National target: 85% of placements through RAA. Best performing RAAs achieve 90%+.
evidence.calculation: 60 placements. If 20 currently interagency at £33K, shifted to RAA at £10K = 20 x £23K = £460K. Plus faster matching reduces time in foster care: £0.5-1.5M total.
evidence.kpi_link: Ofsted adoption scorecard, timeliness of placement (A2 indicator)

implementation_steps.step: Review current RAA vs interagency split and identify reasons for external placements
Increase RAA recruiter capacity (if bottleneck is supply of adopters)
Set 90% RAA target with quarterly monitoring
Renegotiate interagency fee schedule for remaining external placements

evidence.political_framing: We have our own adoption agency. Using it instead of expensive external agencies saves money and finds families faster.

savings_levers.description: Family Group Conferences (FGCs) bring extended families together to create safety plans before court proceedings. Each successful FGC that diverts a child from care saves £25K in court costs plus £30-50K/yr in placement costs. Expanding FGC capacity to 100 additional families per year diverts 40-60 children from care proceedings.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Children Act 1989 s17/s47, Pre-proceedings protocol (PLO), FGC not yet statutory (proposed in C&F Bill)

data_points: Care proceedings cost £25K per case (court, legal, guardian)
evidence.benchmark: Leeds FC&S model achieved 40% reduction in care entries through FGC. Hertfordshire Family Safeguarding model (which includes FGC) reduced care entries by 26%.
evidence.calculation: 100 additional FGCs. 65% success rate = 65 diversions. Court cost avoided: 65 x £25K = £1.6M. Placement cost avoided (year 1): 65 x £35K x 0.5yr = £1.1M. Total: £1-3M.
evidence.kpi_link: Ofsted inspection, care entry rate, PLO timeliness, family outcome measures

implementation_steps.step: Recruit 4 additional FGC coordinators (or commission from partner agency)
Train all child protection teams to refer to FGC before PLO
Deliver first 50 additional FGCs targeting pre-PLO stage
Scale to 100/yr with outcome tracking against care entry rate

evidence.political_framing: Families should solve family problems wherever possible. Family Group Conferences keep children safe with their own families and save £25,000 per case in court costs.

savings_levers.lever: UASC National Transfer Scheme full cost recovery
savings_levers.description: Lancashire accommodates unaccompanied asylum-seeking children above the 0.07% threshold. The Home Office funds a daily rate per child but this rarely covers actual placement costs. Pursuing full cost recovery for all UASC placements above threshold, including back-billing for underfunded periods, recovers £1-2M.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Immigration Act 2016 s69, National Transfer Scheme, Home Office UASC funding instructions

data_points: Home Office daily rate does not cover actual placement costs
Shortfall estimated £5-15K per UASC per year
evidence.benchmark: Kent CC pursued judicial review of Home Office underfunding. Multiple councils now employ specialist UASC cost recovery officers.
evidence.calculation: Estimated 150+ UASC in LCC care. Average shortfall £8K/yr = £1.2M. Back-billing for previous years: additional £0.5-0.8M. Total: £1-2M.
evidence.kpi_link: Home Office reporting, Ofsted UASC inspection, immigration returns data

implementation_steps.step: Audit all UASC placements against actual cost vs Home Office reimbursement
Submit cost recovery claims for shortfalls above threshold
Back-bill for previous underfunded periods (up to 3 years)
Establish quarterly reconciliation with Home Office

evidence.political_framing: The Home Office sends asylum-seeking children to Lancashire but does not pay the full cost. We are billing them for every penny owed. Lancashire taxpayers should not subsidise a broken national system.

operational_context.ofsted_note: From April 2026, Ofsted stops giving overall headline ratings. Individual domain ratings continue.
operational_context.transformation_programme: Family Safeguarding (since 2021, based on Hertfordshire model)

portfolios.title: Resources, HR & Property
directors: Brendan Arnold, Interim Director of Finance and Commerce (Acting S151)
Heloise MacAndrew, Director of Law and Governance (Monitoring Officer)
Jacquie McGeachie, Interim Director of People (HR)
Simon Lawrence, Director of Growth and Property
Sarah Robinson, Head of Service, Property
Clare Joynson, Head of Service, Facilities Management

statutory_duties.summary: S151 officer must ensure proper financial administration
statutory_duties.risk: Cannot remove, statutory officer role. Gary Fielding appointed permanent from Jun 2026.

statutory_duties.summary: Annual accounts preparation and external audit
statutory_duties.risk: Legal requirement

statutory_duties.summary: Duty to appoint officers for proper discharge of functions
statutory_duties.risk: Flexible on structure but must maintain core functions

statutory_duties.summary: Births, deaths and marriages registration
statutory_duties.risk: Statutory service, Home Office funded

statutory_duties.summary: Coroner service provision
statutory_duties.risk: Cannot reduce, joint service with other authorities

statutory_duties.summary: New procurement regulations from 24 February 2025. Dynamic Markets replace DPS.
statutory_duties.risk: Must follow new procedures. Training and system changes required

key_services: Financial management and treasury (total debt £1.2B, £80M/yr service cost)
Property and estate management (1,200+ assets, ~£132M property/estates/facilities = 6.3% of spend)
Pension fund administration (LCPF £10.8B, 115% funded, top 3 nationally)
Procurement (devolved model under review, efficiency review targeting centralisation)
known_pressures: DSG deficit: £22.4M (Mar 2025), forecast £140M (Mar 2026), £328M (Mar 2027), £545M (Mar 2028). Statutory override ends Mar 2028. DfE 90% write-off (HNSG) covers ~£126M of pre-Mar 2026 deficit.
Reserves declining: £326.8M (Mar 2024) to £223.4M forecast (Mar 2026). General fund just £23.4M (2.25%).
Oracle Fusion catastrophe (2022): data breaches, payment delays, salary overpayments, limited assurance audit opinion. KPMG review classified exempt. Now stabilised.
Property estate maintenance backlog (£5M+ for 5 ASC care homes alone)
Pension fund liabilities (though 115% funded)
External audit delays (sector-wide)
S151 transition: Noel O'Neill → Brendan Arnold (interim) → John Betts (interim) → Gary Fielding (permanent, from Jun 2026)
Bond investments ~£350M below purchase value
Multiple interim directors (Finance, People, Strategy), organisational instability

demand_pressures.detail: If statutory override ends Mar 2028 without extension, council cannot set balanced budget. 79% of councils nationally say same. National DSG deficits: £6B (£14B forecast by 2027/28).
6 consecutive years of decline (£420M→£345M→£326.8M→£223.4M forecast). General fund at 2.25%, dangerously close to S114 territory.
Procurement Act 2023 live from Feb 2025. Devolved procurement model means many non-specialist staff doing procurement. Training and system changes needed.
1,200+ assets with unknown total maintenance backlog. 5 ASC care homes alone need £5M+. Active disposal programme but no published estate strategy.

key_contracts.name: Softcat IT infrastructure
key_contracts.note: £10.4M in DOGE spending data (20 months)

key_contracts.name: Local Pensions Partnership
key_contracts.note: Pension fund administration

key_contracts.name: Bloom procurement support
key_contracts.note: 2 contracts, both awarded 0 days after deadline. DOGE weak competition flag.

key_contracts.name: Beever & Struthers audit
key_contracts.note: 94.4% of procurement threshold, threshold-adjacent flag

savings_levers.description: 1,200+ properties. Active disposals: Willow Lane Lancaster (£350K+), Golden Hill, Horncliffe Quarry, Garstang Pool. Property/estates/facilities = 6.3% of spend (~£132M). Every vacant property costs £30-100K/yr to maintain.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: S123 LGA 1972: best consideration requirements for disposal

data_points: Active disposals: Willow Lane (£350K+), Golden Hill, Horncliffe Quarry, Garstang Pool
Simon Lawrence title changed to 'Director of Growth and Property'
evidence.benchmark: Best practice: estate utilisation review every 3 years. Leading councils achieve 20-30% estate reduction through rationalisation
evidence.calculation: 1,200 properties. If 10% surplus/underused = 120 properties × avg £50K/yr running cost = £6M. Disposal receipts additional. Conservative £3-8M including maintenance avoided + receipts
evidence.kpi_link: No inspection metric but LGR preparation requires estate strategy. Pre-LGR rationalisation avoids transferring liabilities to successor authority

implementation_steps.step: Complete full estate condition survey (currently unknown total backlog)
Identify surplus/underutilised properties (target 120+)
Accelerate disposal programme: list 50 properties for sale
Co-location strategy: consolidate services into fewer buildings
Publish estate strategy (currently none published)

evidence.political_framing: We own over 1,200 properties costing £132 million a year. Every empty building is taxpayer money wasted on heating and security. We're selling what we don't need and consolidating what we do.

savings_levers.description: Support services £123M gross. Review internal recharging model. Eliminate duplication between central and directorate-level admin. £11M redundancy provision already in budget.

data_points: £11M redundancy provision in budget (400 estimated jobs)
Multiple interim directors suggest structural instability
Efficiency review initiated June 2025 covering 5 focus areas
evidence.benchmark: LGA peer review recommendation: support service costs should be 8-12% of net revenue. If Lancashire above this, scope for reduction
evidence.calculation: £123M gross. If 5% efficiency through consolidation = £6.2M. After redundancy costs (£11M provision ÷ 3 years = £3.7M/yr amortised): net £2-4M
evidence.kpi_link: Efficiency review already commissioned. Results should inform restructuring. LGR preparation requires lean central services

implementation_steps.step: Complete efficiency review (5 focus areas: procurement, property, digital, workforce, partnerships)
Map all support functions across directorates and identify duplicates
Design consolidated support service model
Implement restructuring using £11M redundancy provision

evidence.political_framing: We've set aside £11 million for redundancy because we know the back office is too big. The efficiency review will tell us exactly where to cut, and we'll act on it.

savings_levers.description: Automate finance processes (e-invoicing, three-way matching, automated reconciliation). Oracle Fusion stabilised after 2022 catastrophe. Reduce headcount through natural attrition.

data_points: Oracle Fusion now stabilised after 2022 catastrophe
100% empty transaction descriptions indicate poor process discipline
Multiple interim directors, but new permanent S151 (Fielding) from Jun 2026 enables change
evidence.benchmark: CIPFA benchmark: finance function costs should be 1.0-1.5% of net revenue. If LCC above this, scope for digitalisation. NHS SBS saved £500M over 10 years through e-invoicing
evidence.calculation: Estimated 80 finance FTEs across directorates. If 20% of work automated through Oracle improvements: 16 FTE × £35K avg = £560K. Including process efficiency and error reduction: £1-3M
evidence.kpi_link: External audit delays (sector-wide) partly caused by manual processes. Automation reduces audit preparation time and improves data quality for Grant Thornton/EY

implementation_steps.step: Wait for Gary Fielding appointment (Jun 2026) to lead transformation
Complete Oracle Fusion process mapping and identify all manual workarounds
Automate top 10 manual finance processes (reconciliation, reporting, variance analysis)
Redeploy finance staff from processing to analysis (natural attrition for headcount reduction)

evidence.political_framing: The Oracle disaster was the previous administration's legacy. We've stabilised it. Now we're going to automate the back office so finance staff can focus on finding savings, not processing paperwork.

savings_levers.description: Three S151 holders in 18 months (O'Neill→Arnold→Betts→Fielding). Gary Fielding arrives from North Yorkshire Jun 2026. Stabilise structure, eliminate interim costs.

data_points: 4 S151 officers in 18 months: O'Neill → Arnold → Betts → Fielding
Interim costs typically 50-100% premium over permanent
Gary Fielding: permanent appointment from Jun 2026 (from North Yorkshire Council)
Bond investments ~£350M below purchase value, needs stable leadership to address
evidence.benchmark: Permanent S151 costs £130-160K/yr. Interim S151 costs £200-300K/yr (day rate + expenses). 4 transitions in 18 months = significant overhead
evidence.calculation: Interim S151 premium ~£100K/yr over permanent. Associated interim directors (Finance, People, Strategy): 3 × £80K premium = £240K. Total interim overhead: £340K. Stabilisation saves £0.5-1M including transition costs
evidence.kpi_link: CIPFA Financial Resilience Index flags leadership instability. Stable S151 enables proper treasury management (£350M bond portfolio needs attention)

implementation_steps.step: Gary Fielding starts June 2026, induction and handover from John Betts
Appoint permanent Director of Finance (replace Brendan Arnold interim)
Complete full financial management structure review
Terminate all interim finance leadership arrangements

evidence.political_framing: Four finance directors in eighteen months is not stability. Gary Fielding is a proven financial leader from North Yorkshire. He will bring the permanent leadership this council needs.

savings_levers.description: Back-office shared services (HR, finance, legal, IT, audit, procurement) with 12 district councils. Nationally, shared service centres have saved £1.34B. LGR preparation makes this natural: start now, formalise at vesting.

data_points: 12 district councils with duplicate back-office functions
LGR vesting Apr 2028 means integration inevitable
Some districts already share services (e.g. Burnley/Pendle joint CEO 2015-2021)
evidence.benchmark: Orbis (Surrey/East Sussex/Brighton): £12.5M annual savings. LGSS (Cambs/Northants): £7.5M before collapse. Successful models achieve 15-25% back-office cost reduction
evidence.calculation: 12 districts × avg £5M back-office spend = £60M total. If LCC provides shared services at 10% lower cost = £6M saving across Lancashire. LCC share: £2-5M through economies of scale
evidence.kpi_link: LGR preparation metric. Shared services now = smoother transition at vesting. Also demonstrates Reform commitment to cross-tier efficiency

implementation_steps.step: Map all 12 district back-office functions and costs
Identify 3 quick-win shared services (payroll, legal, internal audit)
Negotiate shared service agreements with willing districts
Deploy LCC systems (Oracle, Liquid Logic) as shared platform
Formalise into LGR transition plan

evidence.political_framing: Why should 12 district councils each run their own payroll, legal team, and audit function? Sharing back-office services saves millions, and it's exactly what LGR will require anyway.

savings_levers.legal_constraints: s101/s102 LGA 1972: joint arrangements. Requires district agreement (voluntary pre-LGR)

savings_levers.description: Automate invoice processing with purchase order matching and goods receipt confirmation. Eliminates duplicate payments, reduces processing time, improves supplier payment terms.

data_points: £93.6M potential duplicates flagged in DOGE data
Oracle Fusion now stabilised after 2022 catastrophe
100% empty transaction descriptions hamper manual checking
Multiple suppliers with near-identical payment patterns
evidence.benchmark: NHS Shared Business Services: e-invoicing saved £500M over 10 years. Private sector benchmark: 60-80% processing cost reduction
evidence.calculation: Current manual processing estimated 50,000 invoices/month. At £5/invoice manual vs £0.50 automated = £4.50 × 600,000/yr = £2.7M. Conservative £1-2M after implementation costs
evidence.kpi_link: Oracle transparency fix (0.0 score) is prerequisite. E-invoicing forces data quality improvement as side effect

implementation_steps.step: Configure Oracle Fusion three-way matching (PO→GRN→Invoice)
Mandate e-invoicing for top 100 suppliers (80% of spend)
Automated duplicate detection rules in Oracle
Roll out to remaining suppliers

evidence.political_framing: We've flagged £93.6 million in potential duplicate payments. Most are probably data artifacts, but we can't tell because Oracle has zero transaction descriptions. E-invoicing fixes both problems at once.

savings_levers.description: LCC holds average cash balances of £180M. Current investment return is £8.5M. Switching £50M from overnight deposits to money market funds and short-dated gilts could yield an additional £1-3M annually at current base rates. No additional risk: these are DMADF/Treasury-backed instruments.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: CIPFA Treasury Management Code, MHCLG Investment Guidance 2018, S12 LGA 2003

evidence.benchmark: Top-quartile county councils achieve 4.3-4.8% yield on cash balances. LCC yield approximately 4.7% already. Marginal gain from duration extension and instrument mix.
evidence.calculation: £50M moved from overnight to 3-6 month instruments at 0.3-0.5% premium = £150K-250K. Plus £30M into short gilts at 0.8% premium = £240K. Plus renegotiation of bank deposit rates on remaining £100M. Total: £1-3M.
evidence.kpi_link: CIPFA Financial Resilience Index, S151 annual treasury report

implementation_steps.step: Review current investment portfolio and maturity profile with treasury team
Benchmark yields against CIPFA peer group and local authority average
Restructure investment mix: reduce overnight, increase MMFs and short gilts
Report revised Treasury Management Strategy to Full Council

evidence.political_framing: Every pound sitting idle in a low-interest account is a pound not working for Lancashire taxpayers. We are putting council cash to work within safe, government-backed instruments.

savings_levers.description: Local authorities recover VAT under Section 33 VATA 1994. LCC must stay below the 5% partial exemption de minimis threshold. A specialist review of capital schemes, mixed-use properties, and exempt income streams typically recovers £0.5-2M in previously unclaimed VAT. Common wins: leisure centres, property disposals, staff car parking, and traded services.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: VATA 1994 Section 33, HMRC Notice 749, 5% de minimis partial exemption test

data_points: Traded services income across education and highways
evidence.benchmark: Ernst & Young and PwC reviews for similar county councils recover £0.5-2M on first engagement. Hampshire CC recovered £1.4M in 2023 from partial exemption review.
evidence.calculation: Typical recovery: capital scheme VAT (£200-500K), property disposal input tax (£100-300K), mixed-use apportionment correction (£100-400K), traded services output tax review (£50-200K). Range: £0.5-2M.
evidence.kpi_link: Section 151 officer statutory duty, annual audit report, CIPFA FM Code

implementation_steps.step: Commission specialist VAT review (Big 4 or Pstax/VAT Advisors)
Identify unclaimed input tax on capital projects and property transactions
Review partial exemption calculation methodology and de minimis headroom
Submit amended VAT returns for recoverable periods (up to 4 years back)

evidence.political_framing: HMRC owes us money. Previous administrations never commissioned a proper VAT review. We are recovering every penny of tax relief that Lancashire is legally entitled to.

savings_levers.lever: Management delayering and span of control
savings_levers.description: LCC has 6 management layers in most directorates with average span of control of 5.5 staff per manager. Reducing to 5 layers and widening spans to 1:8 removes approximately 80-120 management posts across the organisation. At average management cost of £45K (including on-costs), this saves £3.6-5.4M. Voluntary redundancy and natural wastage over 12 months.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: TUPE, redundancy consultation (s188 TULRCA 1992), 45-day collective consultation for 20+ redundancies

evidence.benchmark: Local Government Association recommends 1:8 span of control. Kent CC achieved £4.2M saving from management delayering in 2022/23. Comparable county councils average 5 layers.
evidence.calculation: 1,800 management posts. Widening span from 5.5 to 8 reduces management requirement by ~30% at lower tiers. Conservative: 80-120 posts at £45K average (salary + on-costs) = £3.6-5.4M. Using £2-5M as net of redundancy costs.
evidence.kpi_link: Workforce indicators, staff survey results, sickness absence rates, service delivery KPIs

implementation_steps.step: Map current management structure across all directorates (layers + spans)
Design target operating model with maximum 5 layers, minimum 1:8 span
Voluntary redundancy/early retirement scheme (use £11M MTFS provision)
Implement restructures directorate by directorate

evidence.political_framing: Too many managers managing managers. We are flattening the structure so frontline workers can get on with their jobs without six layers of approval.

savings_levers.description: LCC pays approximately £8M annually in insurance premiums. Increasing excesses from £250K to £500K on property and liability, and self-insuring motor fleet, reduces premiums by 15-25%. Municipal mutual arrangements with other Lancashire authorities pool catastrophic risk.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Prudent financial management duty, S151 officer approval required for self-insurance

data_points: Claims history data held by insurer
evidence.benchmark: Norfolk CC saved £1.2M by increasing excess and self-insuring fleet. Municipal mutual arrangements (e.g. LAML) reduce premiums by 10-20%.
evidence.calculation: £8M premium. Excess increase saves 10-15% (£0.8-1.2M). Fleet self-insurance saves 5-10% (£0.4-0.8M). Net of increased claims fund provision: £0.5-2M.
evidence.kpi_link: Annual insurance report to Audit Committee, risk register

implementation_steps.step: Commission actuarial review of claims history and optimal excess levels
Market test insurance programme at renewal (check current contract date)
Establish self-insurance fund for fleet and minor property claims
Explore Lancashire-wide municipal mutual with district councils

evidence.political_framing: We are cutting the insurance bill by taking sensible risk in-house instead of paying inflated premiums. The savings go straight to frontline services.

savings_levers.description: LCC currently uses the regulatory method for Minimum Revenue Provision (MRP), charging 4% of outstanding debt annually. Switching to the asset life method spreads charges over the useful life of the asset (typically 25-40 years for buildings, 5-10 for vehicles). This reduces the annual MRP charge by £1-4M without increasing total lifetime cost, just reprofiling it.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: MHCLG MRP Guidance (revised 2024), S21 Local Authorities (Capital Finance) Regulations 2003, Full Council approval required

data_points: Asset life method: 25-40 year buildings, 5-10 year vehicles
evidence.benchmark: Over 70% of councils now use asset life method. Surrey CC saved £3.8M on transition in 2021. CIPFA considers asset life method best practice.
evidence.calculation: £22M MRP at 4% regulatory. Asset life method on same debt: avg 2.5-3% effective rate (longer useful lives). Saving: £22M x (4% - 2.8%) / 4% = £6.6M gross. Conservative £1-4M net after prudent provision.
evidence.kpi_link: Annual MRP statement to Full Council, external audit, CIPFA Prudential Code indicators

implementation_steps.step: Model MRP under asset life method for all borrowing tranches
Prepare revised MRP statement for Full Council approval
Present to Full Council (next scheduled meeting)
Apply from 2026/27 financial year

evidence.political_framing: We are adopting the same accounting method used by 70% of councils. It does not increase total borrowing costs, it just spreads payments sensibly over the life of the asset. The old method was costing Lancashire £4M a year more than necessary.

savings_levers.description: LCC budgets for 100% of establishment posts but averages 8-12% vacancy rate across the organisation. Applying a 3% mandatory vacancy factor to all staffing budgets (excluding frontline social work and care) generates an immediate top-slice. This does not cut posts. It recognises the reality that vacancies always exist and stops departments hoarding the saving.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: S151 officer budget-setting power, no legal barrier to vacancy factor application

evidence.benchmark: Most councils apply 1.5-3% vacancy factor. Birmingham CC applies 3%. Kent CC applies 2.5%. LCC currently applies 0%.
evidence.calculation: £400M staffing budget x 3% vacancy factor = £12M gross. Exclude frontline social work (£80M) = £320M eligible. £320M x 3% = £9.6M. Conservative: £3-8M after allowing for hard-to-fill posts.
evidence.kpi_link: Budget monitoring, vacancy rate tracking, recruitment KPIs

implementation_steps.step: Calculate vacancy rate by directorate from HR data
Apply 3% vacancy factor to all non-exempt staffing budgets from Q2 2026/27
Require 8-week recruitment delay on all non-critical posts
Monitor monthly and adjust factor if service delivery impacted

evidence.political_framing: We are not cutting jobs. We are being honest about the fact that vacancies always exist, and we are putting that money to better use instead of letting departments sit on it.

savings_levers.description: LCC processes over 750,000 transactions. Negotiating 2% early payment discount (payment within 10 days instead of 30) with the top 50 suppliers by value could save £0.5-1.5M annually. Suppliers benefit from improved cash flow. LCC benefits from lower unit costs.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Late Payment of Commercial Debts (Interest) Act 1998, Procurement Act 2023 prompt payment provisions

data_points: Top 50 suppliers account for ~60% of spend
evidence.benchmark: NHS England early payment scheme saves 1.5-2.5% on participating suppliers. Several councils now operate dynamic discounting platforms.
evidence.calculation: Top 50 suppliers: ~£2.1B spend. If 30% participate at 2% discount = £2.1B x 30% x 2% = £12.6M. But most spend is contractually fixed. Realistic: £0.5-1.5M on discretionary and spot purchases.
evidence.kpi_link: Payment performance statistics, supplier satisfaction survey, cash flow forecasting

implementation_steps.step: Identify top 50 suppliers by annual spend from Oracle data
Approach suppliers with early payment discount proposal
Configure Oracle for dynamic discounting on participating suppliers
Monitor uptake and savings quarterly

evidence.political_framing: We pay our suppliers faster, they give us a better price. Everyone wins. The old system made suppliers wait 30 days and we paid full price for the privilege.

savings_levers.description: LCC has 45 legacy PWLB loans at an average rate of 6.8%. Current PWLB certainty rate is 4.5%. Repaying and reborrowing (where PWLB premature repayment penalties allow) or using LOBOs/market alternatives could save £1-4M annually on interest costs.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: PWLB repayment terms, CIPFA Treasury Management Code, S12 LGA 2003

evidence.benchmark: Multiple councils have restructured PWLB debt saving 1-3%. Warwickshire CC saved £2.1M in 2023 through selective repayment and reborrowing.
evidence.calculation: 45 loans at 6.8% avg. If 20 loans (say £400M face value) can be refinanced at 4.5%, saving 2.3% = £9.2M gross. After PWLB premature repayment penalty (typically 50-80% of gain): net £1-4M.
evidence.kpi_link: Treasury management report to Audit Committee, CIPFA Prudential Indicators

implementation_steps.step: Schedule all PWLB loans by maturity, rate, and penalty cost
Model repay-and-reborrow scenarios for each loan tranche
Execute restructuring on loans where net saving exceeds 0.5%
Report revised debt profile to Audit Committee

evidence.political_framing: Lancashire is paying 6.8% interest on loans taken out years ago when rates were higher. We can refinance at 4.5% today and save millions. Any homeowner would do the same.

savings_levers.lever: Council website and digital advertising income
savings_levers.description: LCC websites receive millions of visits annually. The Council Advertising Network (CAN Digital) provides a turnkey solution for public sector digital advertising, placing quality brand advertising on council websites with no development cost. Revenue share model: 50% of advertising income paid directly, or 75% as media-buying credits. Additionally, council email newsletters, parking payment apps, and customer service portals all carry advertising potential. Quick win: can be live within 6-8 weeks of signing.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: LGA 2003 s93 (charging for discretionary services), Data Protection Act 2018/UK GDPR (no personal data shared with advertisers), Advertising Standards Authority codes, council advertising policy required

data_points: LCC websites receive estimated 5-8M visits per year
Council Advertising Network already serves 100+ UK councils
Digital advertising can be live within 6-8 weeks
Zero upfront cost, pure revenue share model
evidence.benchmark: Coventry City Council: GBP 0.2M/yr from digital advertising. Hampshire CC: GBP 0.3M. Bristol CC: GBP 0.15M. Average county council: GBP 0.1-0.3M.
evidence.calculation: 5M pageviews at GBP 2-5 CPM: GBP 10-25K/month gross. 50% revenue share: GBP 60-150K/yr from web alone. Email newsletters: GBP 30-50K. Parking app: GBP 20-40K. Other digital channels: GBP 50-100K. Total: GBP 0.2-0.5M.
evidence.kpi_link: Revenue per 1000 pageviews, click-through rate, advertiser satisfaction, no impact on user experience metrics

implementation_steps.step: Adopt advertising and sponsorship policy (cabinet approval)
Sign concession agreement with CAN Digital or similar provider
Go live with website advertising (6-8 weeks from signing)
Extend to email newsletters, parking portals, customer apps

evidence.political_framing: Every council in the country is doing this. Millions of people visit our website. We should be generating income from it, not leaving money on the table. Zero cost to set up, income from day one.

savings_levers.lever: Naming rights and council asset sponsorship programme
savings_levers.description: LCC owns hundreds of high-profile assets: county hall, libraries, museums, country parks, bridges, car parks, and leisure facilities. A structured naming rights and sponsorship programme sells naming rights for buildings, rooms, events, wifi networks, and public spaces. Conference rooms become 'The BAE Systems Boardroom', wifi becomes 'Powered by Virgin Media', recycling centres become 'sponsored by...' etc. Requires a clear policy with ethical exclusions (gambling, tobacco, payday lending). Quick win: start with internal meeting rooms and conference facilities, expand to public-facing assets.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: LGA 2003 s93/95 (charging and trading powers), Localism Act 2011 general power of competence, advertising standards codes, council sponsorship policy (ethical screen required), planning permission for external signage

data_points: LCC owns 1,200+ property assets across Lancashire
County Hall, 78 libraries, 5 museums, country parks, bridges
Conference facilities used by hundreds of external hirers annually
Wifi networks in all public buildings
evidence.benchmark: LB Barnet: GBP 0.5M from naming rights (The Barnet Copthall leisure centre became 'Allianz Park'). Multiple London boroughs: GBP 0.2-0.8M. Commercial sector: naming rights for venues typically 1-3% of asset value.
evidence.calculation: County Hall conference suites (5 rooms at GBP 10-30K/yr): GBP 50-150K. Library wifi sponsorship (78 libraries at GBP 1-3K): GBP 78-234K. Country park naming (5 parks at GBP 10-30K): GBP 50-150K. Event sponsorship (20 events at GBP 5-15K): GBP 100-300K. Total: GBP 0.3-1M.
evidence.kpi_link: Income per asset, sponsorship renewal rate, public perception survey scores

implementation_steps.step: Draft and adopt sponsorship and naming rights policy with ethical exclusions
Audit all LCC assets for naming rights potential (start with top 50)
Create sponsorship prospectus and rate card
Approach Lancashire's largest businesses (BAE, Lancashire Teaching Hospitals, UCLan, etc.)
Launch wifi sponsorship across all public buildings (quick win)

evidence.political_framing: Every football club, every arena, every transport system sells naming rights. LCC has GBP 600M of property assets sitting there with no commercial income. This is standard practice, not controversial.

operational_context.oracle_status: Stabilised after catastrophic 2022 migration. Data breaches, payment delays, salary overpayments. KPMG review classified exempt. Limited assurance audit opinion.

workforce.agency_note: Multiple directorates relying on agency staff: ASC, SEND, social work. 110 agency EPs alone.

bt_ocl_partnership.note: BT/OCL £400M ICT partnership ended. Replaced by Softcat £64M IT infrastructure contract (4 years from Dec 2025).

operational_context.efficiency_review: Initiated June 2025 by Reform. Five focus areas: procurement, property, digital, workforce, partnerships.
operational_context.s151_timeline: Noel O'Neill → Brendan Arnold (Oct 2025) → John Betts (Feb 2026 interim) → Gary Fielding (Jun 2026 permanent, from North Yorkshire Council)

portfolios.title: Adult Social Care
directors: Matthew Emerson, Director of Strategic Commissioning and Quality
John Williams, Director of Operations (Adults)
Mairead Gill-Mullarkey, Director of Operations (Adults)

scrutiny_committee.name: Health and Adult Services Scrutiny Committee

statutory_duties.summary: Duty to assess and meet eligible care needs. Wellbeing duty. Prevention duty.
statutory_duties.risk: Cannot cut below assessed eligible needs, judicial review risk. CQC now assessing compliance.

statutory_duties.summary: Best interests decisions, DoLS authorisations
statutory_duties.risk: Deprivation of Liberty: unlawful if not authorised. 332,455 applications nationally, only 19% in time.

statutory_duties.summary: Aftercare for detained patients, free and non-means-tested
statutory_duties.risk: s117 aftercare CANNOT be charged for, absolute duty

statutory_duties.summary: Market shaping duty: ensure diverse, sustainable provider market
statutory_duties.risk: 334 care homes, 11,800 beds. 10% vacancy rate. If major provider fails, council is provider of last resort.

key_services: Residential and nursing care (OP residential £242M, nursing £122M over 20 months, 413 providers, 184 providers)
Home care and reablement (88,000 hours/week commissioned, 31% off-framework at ~20% higher rates)
LD Supported Living (£146M over 20 months, 42 suppliers, ~700 properties, ~1,830 people, £70M/yr)
Day services (5 day centres under review, 3 operating below 60% capacity, 2 closed since pandemic)
Safeguarding adults (all referrals reviewed by end of working day, no waiting list)
Direct payments and personal budgets (£161M over 20 months: 64% more activities, 66% more control)
DoLS (large backlog of unauthorised deprivations. National: 123,790 uncompleted, avg 144 days processing)
In-house care homes (16 total, 5 under review, £5M maintenance backlog. Leader confirmed no closures Feb 2026)
In-house domiciliary services (9 district teams, mostly CQC Good)
Short break/respite services (7 teams, 2 rated Outstanding)
Shared Lives scheme (CQC Outstanding, Lancashire has 9% of national LD Shared Lives placements)
Equipment/telecare (10,000+ pieces issued monthly, no waiting list)
known_pressures: CQC 'Requires Improvement' (Aug 2025): 2/4 across all 9 quality statements. 2,000+ waiting for assessments (up to 226 days), 3,500+ waiting annual reviews (some years late), 1,100 waiting OT (max 262 days)
MTFS target: £46.7M over 2 years (45% of all council savings). 2024/25 target was £34.8M, only £3.8M achieved (£31M shortfall). Overspent by £21.6M.
Demographic growth: over-65 population rising 2.1% p.a. Life expectancy gap: males 10.6 years, females 9.6 years in most deprived wards
National Living Wage impact: NLW increase 6.7% (£11.44→£12.21/hr from Apr 2025) + Employers NI from 13.8%→15%. Providers need 7-8% uplift.
Care market sustainability: 334 homes, 10% vacancy. Fair Cost of Care gap: residential £137/week below fair cost. Agency charges >£50/hour.
Hospital discharge delays: NHS cost shunt to social care. CHC disputes with ICB (the 'daily fight')
CHC disputes: BCF in excess of £200M p.a. ICB is largest funder. £28M savings target for 2024/25 through better cost sharing 'not worked out as preferred'
Strained NHS partnerships: CQC found 'some organisations had disengaged'. Historic tensions and funding disputes block integration.
Leadership instability: Helen Coombes interim since Dec 2024. Permanent DASS recruitment linked to CE recruitment.
In-house care homes: 5 reviewed (229 beds), £5M maintenance backlog. Day centres: 2 still closed since pandemic.

demand_pressures.detail: 2.1% p.a. growth in Lancashire. Ageing population driving residential, nursing and home care demand. Life expectancy gap widening in deprived wards.
NLW +6.7%, Employers NI +1.2pp, CQC fees up, energy costs. Providers need 7-8% fee uplift. Fair Cost of Care: residential £137/week below fair cost.
Requires Improvement rating means CQC will return. Must reduce waiting lists (2,000+ assessments, 3,500+ reviews, 1,100 OT). Post-CQC recruitment: 53 SWs, 14 OTs, 18 SCOs saving £1.6M agency costs.
42 suppliers for £146M (20 months). Average £3.5M per supplier. Any major provider failure could be catastrophic.
Leading cause of sickness absence among ASC staff. LPS implementation expected H1 2026 consultation, 7 years after legislation passed.

key_contracts.name: Home care framework (all zones)
key_contracts.provider: 30+ providers inc. Guardian Homecare (largest, £1.9M/month), Routes Healthcare, Creative Support
key_contracts.note: Zone-based, aligned to NHS Neighbourhood Teams. 31% off-framework at ~20% premium.

key_contracts.name: LD Supported Living
key_contracts.note: 80% quality / 20% price evaluation. 700 properties, 1,830 people.

key_contracts.name: OP Residential (spot purchase)
key_contracts.note: Any CQC-registered provider accepting LCC rates.

key_contracts.name: BCF Pooled Budget
key_contracts.note: ICB is largest single supplier relationship (£170.7M in DOGE data). Chorley & SR BCF: £122M across 24 transactions.

key_contracts.name: Prevention/Reablement block
key_contracts.note: Short-term care at home.

key_contracts.name: LRL PFI legacy
key_contracts.note: Old PFI arrangement, review terms.

savings_levers.description: Systematic review of NHS Continuing Healthcare eligibility. BCF >£200M p.a. The ICB CEO acknowledged whether ICB should 'contribute more'. Establish dedicated CHC review team.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: National Framework for NHS CHC 2022: joint dispute resolution process

data_points: ICB is largest single supplier (£170.7M in DOGE data)
£28M savings target through better cost sharing 'not worked out as preferred'
evidence.benchmark: National CHC recovery rate 8-12%, Lancashire estimated 4-6%
evidence.calculation: 180 high-cost cases × £28K/yr average × 30-50% recovery = £1.5-2.5M. System-wide review of all borderline cases across 334 care homes + home care: £5-12M
evidence.kpi_link: CQC 'Requires Improvement' found strained NHS partnerships, 'some organisations had disengaged'. This blocks CHC disputes. Improvement plan should include reset of ICB relationship

implementation_steps.step: Establish dedicated CHC review team (2 nurses + 1 case manager)
Audit top 50 most expensive jointly-funded packages
Submit systematic claims to ICB using National Framework dispute resolution
Quarterly review cycle for all packages >£1,500/week
Escalate unresolved disputes to NHS England regional team

evidence.political_framing: The NHS should pay its fair share. Lancashire taxpayers are subsidising the ICB. The BCF is over £200M. We need proper accountability for how that money is spent.

article_refs.title: The Truth About Lancashire's Care Homes

savings_levers.description: 31% of home care commissioned off-framework at ~20% higher rates. Move to framework providers. New zone-based contracts from Nov 2023 should enable this.

data_points: 30+ providers on framework inc. Guardian Homecare (£1.9M/month)
evidence.benchmark: Best practice councils <15% off-framework. Some achieve <10%
evidence.calculation: 31% of 88K hrs/week = 27,280 hrs off-framework. At 20% premium over ~£20/hr = £5.46/hr excess × 27,280 × 52 weeks = £7.7M excess. Reducing to 15% = £3-5M saving
evidence.kpi_link: CQC found assessment waiting lists (2,000+) force emergency off-framework placements. Reducing backlogs = more planned placements on framework

implementation_steps.step: Map all off-framework packages by zone, provider, and reason for off-framework
Review zone-based framework capacity and identify gaps causing off-framework
Targeted recruitment of framework providers in underserved zones
Transfer existing off-framework packages to framework at next review point

evidence.political_framing: We're paying 20% more for the same care because the previous administration didn't manage contracts properly. Framework providers deliver the same quality at lower cost.

savings_levers.description: Lancashire reablement success: 87.3% still at home at 91 days (national 81.8%). But only 2.6% offered reablement after discharge (national 2.8%). Every 1% shift from residential to community saves ~£3M.

data_points: Positive Ageing Mental Health Trailblazer achieves 4 weeks earlier discharge
In-house domiciliary teams: 9 districts, mostly CQC Good
evidence.benchmark: National reablement offer rate 2.8%, top performers 4-5%. Lancashire 2.6%
evidence.calculation: 2.6% → 4% offer rate on ~15,000 hospital discharges/yr = 210 additional people through reablement. At 87.3% success rate, 183 avoid residential (£650/week avg). 183 × £650 × 52 = £6.2M avoided. Net of reablement cost (~£3M) = £3-5M
evidence.kpi_link: CQC found assessment backlogs delay reablement offers. Reducing waits means more people get timely reablement before they deteriorate into needing residential

implementation_steps.step: Expand in-house reablement capacity by 20% (recruit 30 additional staff)
Embed reablement pathway at point of hospital discharge as default offer
Train NHS discharge coordinators on reablement referral criteria
Monitor 91-day outcomes and adjust capacity by zone

evidence.political_framing: Lancashire's reablement service is outstanding, with an 87% success rate. We just need to offer it to more people. It keeps people independent and saves the taxpayer millions.

savings_levers.description: Remote monitoring, telecare, digital care planning. £3M allocated over 3 years for digital transformation. Reduces face-to-face visits for stable clients.

data_points: £3M allocated over 3 years for digital transformation
10,000+ pieces telecare equipment issued monthly, no waiting list
Liquid Logic social care system integration challenges
evidence.benchmark: Leading councils (Kent, Hampshire) achieve 15-20% reduction in face-to-face visits through remote monitoring
evidence.calculation: 88,000 hours/week home care. If 10% of stable clients shift to remote monitoring (reduced visits), saving 1-2 hours/week each × 8,800 clients × 1.5 hrs × £20/hr × 52 = £1-2M
evidence.kpi_link: CQC 'unreliable electronic records' finding means digital transformation is mandated by improvement plan. Investment in digital care tech addresses CQC AND saves money

implementation_steps.step: Identify stable home care clients suitable for remote monitoring (target 2,000)
Procure and deploy remote monitoring devices and platform
Reduce scheduled visits for monitored clients (from daily to 3x/week)
Reinvest freed capacity into reducing assessment waiting list

evidence.political_framing: Technology keeps people safer at home while freeing up care workers for those who need them most. It's modern, it's efficient, and it improves outcomes.

savings_levers.description: Self-directed support typically 15-20% cheaper. CQC found direct payments deliver 64% more activities, 66% more control, 60% better health. Increase uptake.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Care Act 2014 s31-33: must offer DPs to all eligible

data_points: 64% more activities, 66% more control, 60% better health (CQC)
Self-directed support 15-20% cheaper than commissioned care
evidence.benchmark: National DP take-up varies 20-40%. Leading councils achieve 35%+
evidence.calculation: If DP take-up increases by 10pp across 5,000 eligible clients, 500 shift. At 15% saving on avg £400/week package = £60/week × 500 × 52 = £1.56M. Higher-value packages yield more
evidence.kpi_link: CQC assessed Lancashire on personalisation. DPs are the key metric. Increasing uptake simultaneously improves CQC rating AND saves money

implementation_steps.step: Identify all eligible clients not currently offered DPs
Simplify DP administration (prepaid card, managed accounts)
Train social workers to default-offer DPs at every assessment/review
Peer support network for DP holders

evidence.political_framing: Direct payments give people control over their own care. They choose better, cheaper options than the council would commission. It's personal freedom AND value for money.

savings_levers.description: OP residential £172M/yr annualised vs home care £57M/yr. If 10% of residential placements converted to home care = £10-15M savings. Living Better Lives programme targets this.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Care Act 2014: must demonstrate placement meets assessed needs

data_points: OP residential £242M (20 months) = £172M/yr annualised via 413 providers
Fair Cost of Care gap: residential £137/week below fair cost
Living Better Lives programme already underway
evidence.benchmark: National direction is community-first. Best-performing councils achieve 60:40 community:residential (Lancashire currently ~25:75)
evidence.calculation: 10% of residential placements (~1,100 beds × 10% = 110 people) moved from residential (avg £800/week) to enhanced home care (avg £350/week) = £450/week × 110 × 52 = £2.6M. At 20% shift: £5.1M. Including Extra Care Housing and Shared Lives: £5-10M
evidence.kpi_link: CQC improvement plan requires strength-based practice (Living Better Lives). Every person maintained in community with right support = better CQC rating AND lower cost

implementation_steps.step: Review all residential placements at next annual review and identify step-down candidates
Expand Extra Care Housing capacity (commission 100 additional units)
Expand Shared Lives scheme (Outstanding rated, 9% of national LD placements)
Commission enhanced home care packages with night support for complex needs
Invest in community equipment/adaptations to enable home living

evidence.political_framing: People want to live at home, not in care homes. We spend 3x more on residential than home care. Shifting the balance is better for people AND for taxpayers.

article_refs.title: The Truth About Lancashire's Care Homes

savings_levers.description: Every £1 in falls prevention saves £3.50 in ASC. Cross-portfolio with Public Health. Positive Ageing Mental Health Trailblazer already achieves 4 weeks earlier discharge.

data_points: Every £1 in falls prevention saves £3.50 in ASC (PHE evidence)
Positive Ageing Mental Health Trailblazer: 4 weeks earlier discharge
Falls are the leading cause of emergency hospital admission for older people
evidence.benchmark: PHE evidence base: £1 invested = £3.50 saved. NICE guideline CG161 recommends multifactorial falls assessment for all at-risk
evidence.calculation: Estimated 3,000 falls-related ASC admissions/yr in Lancashire. If 20% prevented through targeted intervention = 600 fewer admissions × avg £8K residential cost = £4.8M avoided. Investment: £1.5M = net £2-5M
evidence.kpi_link: CQC improvement demands prevention focus. Falls prevention is the single highest-ROI preventive intervention in ASC. Cross-portfolio with Public Health grant (ring-fenced)

implementation_steps.step: Commission falls risk screening for all 75+ at GP registration/annual review
Expand strength and balance classes in all 12 districts (Otago exercise programme)
Home hazard assessment and modification programme
Monitor hospital admission data to track impact

evidence.political_framing: Prevention is always cheaper than cure. For every pound we invest in falls prevention, we save three pounds fifty in care costs. It keeps people on their feet and out of care homes.

savings_levers.lever: Brokerage team expansion for care purchasing
savings_levers.description: Create a centralised brokerage team to negotiate all care package placements. Currently social workers arrange packages individually with no volume leverage. A brokerage team aggregates demand and negotiates 5-10% lower unit costs with providers.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Care Act 2014 duty to meet assessed needs, provider market sustainability duty

evidence.benchmark: Hertfordshire CC brokerage team reduced average placement cost by 8%. Kent CC achieved 6% reduction through centralised purchasing.
evidence.calculation: £584M total. If brokerage covers £300M of direct care purchasing and achieves 3-5% cost reduction = £9-15M. Conservative: £2-5M in year 1 as team builds capability.
evidence.kpi_link: CQC inspection, ASCOF metrics, unit cost benchmarking

implementation_steps.step: Recruit brokerage manager and 4-6 brokers (or redeploy from commissioning)
Map all current placements and unit costs by provider and care type
Negotiate block contracts and volume discounts with top 20 providers
Route all new placements through brokerage from month 6

evidence.political_framing: Social workers should be caring for people, not haggling over prices. A specialist purchasing team gets better deals and frees up social workers to do their real job.

savings_levers.description: Direct payments give service users cash to arrange their own care. They are typically 15% cheaper than council-commissioned packages because users hire personal assistants at lower rates. LCC take-up is below the England average. Shifting 500 clients to direct payments saves £1-3M.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Care Act 2014 s31-33, mandatory offer duty, Mental Capacity Act considerations

data_points: Current direct payment take-up below England average
evidence.benchmark: England average take-up 28%. Top quartile 35%. Several councils run 'DP champions' in each team to boost referrals.
evidence.calculation: 500 clients x (£22K - £18.7K) saving = 500 x £3,300 = £1.65M. If 300-600 shift achieved: £1-2M. Including reduced commissioning overhead: £1-3M.
evidence.kpi_link: ASCOF 1C (proportion receiving direct payments), CQC inspection

implementation_steps.step: Identify eligible clients currently on commissioned packages
Train social workers and appoint DP champions per team
Run promotional campaign targeting eligible clients and carers
Provide support broker for first 3 months of each new DP

evidence.political_framing: Direct payments put people in charge of their own care. They choose who helps them, when, and how. It costs less and delivers better outcomes. That is Reform in action.

savings_levers.description: Shared Lives places adults with learning disabilities or mental health needs with approved carers in their own homes. It costs £25-30K/yr vs £60-80K for residential care. Recruiting 50 additional Shared Lives carers and placing 50 people diverts them from residential at £30-50K saving per placement.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: CQC registration for Shared Lives scheme, Care Act 2014 assessed needs

data_points: Current Shared Lives scheme has limited capacity
evidence.benchmark: South Gloucestershire expanded by 40 carers saving £1.6M. Shared Lives Plus reports 97% rated Good/Outstanding by CQC.
evidence.calculation: 50 placements x (£65K residential - £28K Shared Lives) = 50 x £37K = £1.85M. Net of recruitment and support costs: £1-2M.
evidence.kpi_link: ASCOF, CQC inspection, learning disability mortality review (LeDeR)

implementation_steps.step: Review current Shared Lives capacity and waiting list
Recruitment campaign for 50 additional carers across Lancashire
Match and place first cohort (target 25 in year 1)
Full 50 placements operational by month 24

evidence.political_framing: Shared Lives gives people a real home, not a care home. It costs half as much and delivers twice the dignity. This is how Reform does social care.

savings_levers.lever: Section 117 aftercare audit and NHS cost recovery
savings_levers.description: Section 117 Mental Health Act aftercare is jointly funded 50/50 with the NHS. Many councils carry the full cost because they never billed the ICB for the NHS share. An audit of all s117 placements to confirm NHS contribution is being received, and back-billing where it is not, typically recovers £1-3M.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: MHA 1983 s117, R (Stennett) v Manchester City Council [2002] (aftercare is free, but NHS must co-fund)

data_points: Estimated 800+ s117 clients in LCC area
No systematic audit of s117 funding splits
evidence.benchmark: Birmingham CC recovered £2.8M from s117 audit in 2022. Cornwall CC recovered £1.5M. Standard savings play for any council with MH services.
evidence.calculation: 800 placements x £45K = £36M total. NHS 50% share = £18M. If 10-15% underpaid by NHS = £1.8-2.7M. Plus back-billing for previous years: £1-3M total.
evidence.kpi_link: Joint commissioning board, ICB partnership agreement

implementation_steps.step: Extract all s117 placements from case management system
Cross-reference with ICB funding records to identify gaps
Issue back-billing notices to ICB for underpayment
Establish ongoing reconciliation process with ICB finance team

evidence.political_framing: The NHS is legally required to pay half the cost of mental health aftercare. In many cases, Lancashire taxpayers have been picking up the full bill. We are recovering what is owed.

savings_levers.description: Every telecare installation that prevents or delays a residential admission saves £30-35K per year. Installing 2,000 additional telecare units (pendant alarms, motion sensors, medication dispensers) at £500 per unit (£1M capital) prevents an estimated 300-400 residential admissions.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Care Act 2014 assessed needs, TEC quality standards, data protection for monitoring

data_points: Current telecare coverage below peer average
evidence.benchmark: Leeds CC scaled to 15,000 telecare units, preventing 600 residential admissions/yr. Barnet deployed 3,000 units saving £4M.
evidence.calculation: 2,000 units x £500 = £1M capital. Prevention rate 15-20% = 300-400 admissions avoided x £35K = £10.5-14M gross. Year 1 partial effect: £2-5M net.
evidence.kpi_link: ASCOF delayed transfers of care, permanent admissions per 100K, CQC rating

implementation_steps.step: Procure telecare equipment (£1M capital, funded from DFG or capital programme)
Train assessment teams to prescribe telecare as default first intervention
Install first 500 units targeting highest-risk clients (frequent fallers, dementia)
Scale to 2,000 units by month 18, monitor admission rates monthly

evidence.political_framing: A £500 sensor in someone's home keeps them independent and out of a £35,000 care placement. Technology first, residential care last.

operational_context.cqc_score: 2 out of 4 across all 9 quality statements
cqc_key_findings: Mental health leading cause of staff sickness
cqc_improvements_since: Agency staff reduction saving £1.6M since April
operational_context.transformation_programme: Living Better Lives in Lancashire (LBLiL): strength-based practice, three-step conversation, digital ASC, wellbeing and early support function
operational_context.conflict_of_interest: Cabinet Member Graham Dalton is part-owner of 1st for Care GB (Lancaster, private care). Protestations of no interest challenged at scrutiny committee.

in_house_care_homes.review_outcome: Leader confirmed no closures (Feb 2026). Investment pledged. Full report to Cabinet April 2026.

operational_context.shared_lives: Outstanding, 9% of total national LD Shared Lives placements. Crown jewel of LCC ASC.

cross_portfolio_dependencies.dependency: Falls prevention investment in Public Health saves £3.50 per £1 in ASC residential avoidance
Digital care technology and self-service portals reduce ASC operational costs
Property rationalisation affects care home estate. Procurement reform for off-framework home care.

portfolios.title: Health & Wellbeing
directors: Dr Sakthi Karunanithi, Director of Public Health, Wellbeing and Communities
Clare Coupe, Director of Communities and Engagement

scrutiny_committee.name: Health and Adult Services Scrutiny Committee

statutory_duties.summary: Director of Public Health, statutory chief officer. Public health functions.
statutory_duties.risk: DPH is statutory role, cannot remove

statutory_duties.summary: Duty to take steps to improve public health
statutory_duties.risk: Broad duty, discretion on how to deliver

statutory_duties.summary: Health protection and disease control
statutory_duties.risk: Cannot reduce emergency preparedness

key_services: Drug and alcohol services (CGL Services, £36.3M combined across 2 entity names)
Health visiting and school nursing (0-19, HCRG Care Services £36.7M combined, 85% of category spend)
known_pressures: HCRG Care Services near-monopoly: HHI 3,615 (highest of any budget category). 85% of spend through single provider (formerly Virgin Care). £36.7M combined across 2 entity names.
CGL dual entity: appears as 'CGL' and 'CGL Services Ltd'. £36.3M combined. Rebilling patterns (£561K x15 = £8.4M).
Public Health Grant ring-fenced but flat in real terms, constrains how money can be used
Drug and alcohol demand increasing post-COVID. National drug strategy funding uncertain.
Mental health demand, particularly young people. Suicide prevention critical.
Health inequalities widening in deprived Lancashire wards
5.4% round number rate, highest of any budget category (suggests estimates/block amounts rather than invoiced costs)
0% contract coverage on Contracts Finder. No public health contracts published. Major transparency gap.

demand_pressures.detail: 85% of category spend through single provider. If HCRG exits or fails, the 0-19 health visiting service collapses. No realistic alternative at this scale without major recommissioning.
