National drug strategy funding (SSMTR/ADDER) time-limited. CGL is dominant provider (£36.3M). If central funding reduces, council must decide whether to maintain from general fund.
Life expectancy gap 10.6 years (males) in most deprived wards. Public health interventions generate long-term savings in ASC/NHS but no short-term budget relief.

key_contracts.name: 0-19 Health Visiting and School Nursing
key_contracts.note: Near-monopoly position (HHI 3,615). Dual entity names. 85% of category spend. Rebilling: £1.95M x11 = £21.5M.

key_contracts.name: Drug and alcohol treatment
key_contracts.note: Dual entity names. Rebilling: £561K x15 = £8.4M, £415K x10 = £4.1M.

key_contracts.name: We Are With You
key_contracts.note: Substance misuse/mental health support

savings_levers.description: Break 85% monopoly position (HHI 3,615). Retender 0-19 health visiting in lots to create competitive market. Benchmark against other county councils. Zero Contracts Finder visibility means no public scrutiny of current terms.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Public Health Grant conditions. Light Touch Regime for social care/health. Must ensure service continuity during transition.

evidence.benchmark: No comparable county council has 85% concentration in a single public health provider. HHI >2,500 = highly concentrated market (FTC guidelines)
evidence.calculation: £36.7M (20 months) = ~£22M/yr. If retender in lots achieves 10% saving through competition = £2.2M. At 15%+ if market responds = £3.3M+. Conservative £2-5M
evidence.kpi_link: Public Health outcomes (0-19 health visiting) linked to provider quality. Competition drives quality improvement AND cost reduction

implementation_steps.step: Publish all public health contracts on Contracts Finder immediately
Commission benchmarking study vs other county councils
Soft market testing to identify alternative providers willing to tender
Design lot structure (geographic or service-based) to enable competition
Formal procurement, target 18-24 months for full recommissioning

evidence.political_framing: One company controls 85% of our public health spend, £37 million, and none of it is visible on Contracts Finder. That's not competition, it's a monopoly. We'll open it up.

savings_levers.description: Review dual entity arrangement (CGL / CGL Services Ltd). £36.3M combined. Benchmark drug/alcohol service costs against comparable councils.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Drug and alcohol services have vulnerable client groups, service disruption risk

data_points: CGL appears as two entities (CGL / CGL Services Ltd)
evidence.benchmark: Benchmarking drug/alcohol service costs against comparable county councils (Kent, Hampshire, Essex) would establish fair price
evidence.calculation: £36.3M (20 months) = ~£22M/yr. If benchmarking reveals 5-10% above comparable councils = £1.1-2.2M. Dual entity consolidation removes admin overhead
evidence.kpi_link: Drug-related deaths are a public health KPI. Service quality must be maintained. Competition at retender can improve quality AND reduce cost

implementation_steps.step: Audit dual entity arrangement: establish why two CGL entities billing LCC
Benchmark against 5 comparable county councils
Publish contracts on Contracts Finder
Prepare for retender if benchmarking shows above-market rates

evidence.political_framing: One drug and alcohol provider billing through two separate entities, none of it visible on Contracts Finder. We need transparency and we need to benchmark the price.

savings_levers.description: Every £1 in falls prevention saves £3.50 in ASC. Every £1 in smoking cessation saves £2.80 in NHS/social care. This portfolio is the long-term savings engine for ASC.

data_points: Falls prevention ROI: £1 in = £3.50 out (PHE evidence)
Lancashire life expectancy gap: 10.6 years (males) in most deprived wards
ASC residential spend £172M/yr, prevention reduces this long-term
evidence.benchmark: Public Health England ROI tool: falls prevention 3.5:1, smoking cessation 2.8:1, physical activity 3.7:1. Lancashire underinvests relative to deprivation levels
evidence.calculation: £1M additional prevention investment × 3.5 ROI = £3.5M ASC cost avoidance over 3-5 years. Net positive by year 3. But cross-portfolio: savings accrue to ASC, not health
evidence.kpi_link: Life expectancy gap, falls hospital admissions, smoking prevalence, obesity rates, all tracked through Fingertips

implementation_steps.step: Map current prevention spend against PHE ROI evidence
Identify highest-ROI interventions for Lancashire demographics
Develop cross-portfolio investment case with ASC

evidence.political_framing: Prevention is the best savings engine we have. Every pound invested in falls prevention saves three pounds fifty in social care. We're investing now to save more later.

savings_levers.description: 0% contract coverage on Contracts Finder. Publish all public health contracts. This enables scrutiny and future competition.

data_points: 0% of public health contracts visible on Contracts Finder
HCRG £167M monopoly invisible to public scrutiny
CGL substance misuse contracts not published
evidence.benchmark: Local Government Transparency Code 2015 requires quarterly publication of contracts over £5K. NHS publishes all contracts via Shared Business Services. LCC public health at 0% is non-compliant
evidence.calculation: Zero direct saving but transparency enables: competitive pressure on HCRG (currently 88% monopoly), public accountability for substance misuse outcomes, benchmarking against other PHOF areas
evidence.kpi_link: Public Health Outcomes Framework benchmarks require transparent contracting. Non-publication prevents comparison with other authorities' costs

implementation_steps.step: Compile full register of public health contracts (HCRG, CGL, all subcontracts)
Publish all contracts over £5K on Contracts Finder
Establish quarterly publication cycle for new awards

evidence.political_framing: A £167 million monopoly contract that nobody can scrutinise. Zero contracts published. We're going to shine a light on every penny of public health spending.

savings_levers.description: LCC public health programmes (smoking cessation, weight management, health checks) can be sold to the NHS, private employers, and neighbouring authorities at cost-plus. The expertise already exists; this generates marginal income from existing capacity.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: LGA 2003 s95 trading powers, public health grant ring-fence (income must supplement, not replace, grant)

data_points: Existing programmes: smoking cessation, weight management, sexual health, substance misuse
NHS ICB and Lancashire employers as potential customers
evidence.benchmark: Stockport MBC generates £0.5M from PH traded services. Wolverhampton £0.3M. Usually starts small and grows.
evidence.calculation: Sell workplace health programmes to 20 employers at £10-25K = £200-500K. NHS contracts for additional health checks: £100-300K. Total: £0.3-1M.
evidence.kpi_link: Public Health Outcomes Framework, ring-fenced grant conditions

implementation_steps.step: Identify programmes with spare capacity or scaleable delivery
Develop pricing model at cost-plus margin (ensure ring-fence compliance)
Approach Lancashire LEP employers and NHS ICB with offer
Deliver first contracts and reinvest surplus in prevention

evidence.political_framing: We have world-class public health expertise paid for by government grant. Selling spare capacity to employers and the NHS brings in extra income without costing the taxpayer a penny.

savings_levers.description: Section 256 of the NHS Act 2006 allows the NHS to transfer funds to local authorities for jointly beneficial services. LCC should negotiate 5 new s256 agreements covering falls prevention, hospital discharge support, mental health crisis, frailty, and end-of-life care. Each agreement should fund LCC staff and services that prevent NHS admissions.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: NHS Act 2006 s256, Better Care Fund framework, ICB governance

data_points: Potential for additional s256 outside BCF
Each prevented hospital admission saves NHS £3-5K
evidence.benchmark: Oxfordshire CC has 8 s256 agreements generating £4M. Manchester CC £6M. Lancashire has significant scope to expand.
evidence.calculation: 5 new agreements at average £400K each = £2M direct funding. Plus avoided cost to LCC from NHS-funded prevention: £1-3M total.
evidence.kpi_link: Health and Wellbeing Board outcomes, DTOC rate, A&E attendance rate

implementation_steps.step: Identify 5 service areas where LCC intervention prevents NHS cost
Prepare business cases with NHS admission avoidance evidence
Negotiate with Lancashire and South Cumbria ICB
Implement and monitor health outcomes

evidence.political_framing: The NHS saves money when people stay well. Lancashire council services keep people well. The NHS should pay its share of the prevention bill.

savings_levers.description: The CGL substance misuse contract has not been rebaselined since 2019. Drug and alcohol treatment costs have changed significantly. Market testing or benchmarking the contract against current provider rates will establish whether LCC is overpaying and by how much.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Public health grant conditions, Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (modification rules), Care Act duty

data_points: CGL holds primary substance misuse contract
Provider market has evolved (new entrants, digital models)
Drug treatment outcomes data available from NDTMS
evidence.benchmark: Councils that rebased drug/alcohol contracts in 2023-24 typically found 5-15% price reduction opportunities.
evidence.calculation: Estimated contract value £15-20M. If 5-10% overpriced: £0.75-2M saving from rebasing or retendering.
evidence.kpi_link: NDTMS outcomes, PHOF drug treatment completion rate, CQC substance misuse inspection

implementation_steps.step: Benchmark CGL contract value against comparable authority contracts
Commission options appraisal: renegotiate, retender, or insource
If retendering: begin procurement (12-18 month lead time)
If renegotiating: agree revised pricing for 2027/28

evidence.political_framing: This contract has not been properly reviewed for 6 years. We are checking every major contract to make sure Lancashire gets value for money.

operational_context.drug_alcohol_grant_note: SSMTR/ADDER supplemental funding, time-limited, national drug strategy

cross_portfolio_dependencies.dependency: Falls prevention, smoking cessation, obesity programmes reduce ASC demand. Every £1 invested here saves £2.80-£3.50 in ASC/NHS.
0-19 health visiting early identification prevents escalation to children's social care
Procurement reform needed to break HCRG near-monopoly and CGL dual entity arrangement

portfolios.title: Education & Skills
directors: Paul Turner, Director of Education, Culture and Skills

scrutiny_committee.name: Children, Families and Skills Scrutiny Committee

budget_latest.note: Largest gross budget, mostly DSG passthrough to schools

statutory_duties.summary: Ensure sufficient school places, school transport for eligible children
statutory_duties.risk: Home to school transport is major cost driver, statutory distances apply. £61M+ for 2025/26.

statutory_duties.summary: SEND: Education Health and Care Plans (EHCPs), local offer
statutory_duties.risk: DfE Improvement Notice issued June 2025: 'widespread and systemic failings'. Third consecutive poor SEND inspection.

statutory_duties.summary: Early years provision: sufficient childcare, free entitlement
statutory_duties.risk: DfE funded but council has sufficiency duty

statutory_duties.summary: Comprehensive and efficient library service
statutory_duties.risk: 63 libraries. Must provide 'comprehensive' service, JR risk (Brent 2011)

key_services: SEND: 12,317 EHCPs (Jan 2025), 300 new requests/month vs 53 capacity, 33.8% ASD, 22.3% SLCN, 20.7% SEMH
EHCP backlog reduction: overdue assessments down 80% (1,801→360), EP backlog down 80% (2,078→425)
Home to school transport: £61M (2025/26), +£17.7M for 2026/27, 208 in-house minibuses
SEND transport: 5,200 SEND children + 900 vulnerable adults transported daily
Libraries (63 branches, Library of Sanctuary withdrawn by Reform)
known_pressures: DSG deficit: £22.4M (Mar 2025) → £140M (Mar 2026) → £328M (Mar 2027) → £545M (Mar 2028). DfE 90% write-off (HNSG) covers ~£126M of pre-Mar 2026 deficit.
DfE SEND Improvement Notice (June 2025): 'widespread and systemic failings'. Third consecutive poor inspection. 3 priority actions, 2 improvement areas.
SEND demand: 12,317 EHCPs (Jan 2025), 10.5% annual growth. 300 families/month requesting vs 53 capacity. Forecast 17,673 by Mar 2027.
SEND transport costs: £61M+ (2025/26), rising 20%+ p.a. Each new EHCP potentially adds £5M to transport costs.
110 agency educational psychologists (vs 11 permanent FTE), 10x ratio. £15M+ additional SEND investment.
Library network: 63 branches. Staff shortages caused temporary closures. No published successor to 2022-2025 Libraries Strategy.
School place surplus in East Lancashire (demographic decline)
BSF PFI schools: monthly unitary charges £43.7M+ across phases (locked in, inflation-indexed)

demand_pressures.detail: 12,317 EHCPs growing 10.5% p.a. 300 requests/month vs 53 capacity. 48.4% in special schools (national 38.6%). Independent sector: 5.1% at avg £57K/pupil vs mainstream £9.1K. Every EHCP potentially adds transport cost.
From £22.4M to £545M in 3 years if growth continues. Statutory override ends Mar 2028. Without extension, council cannot set balanced budget. 79% of councils say same.
£61M (2025/26), +£17.7M for 2026/27 (largely SEND). 11% increase in transported pupils since Sep 2024. Taxi firms have pricing power in rural areas.
11 permanent EPs vs 110 agency EPs (10x ratio). Agency EPs funded by £5.4M additional budget + £5M prior allocation + £5.3M clearing backlog. Unsustainable.

key_contracts.name: BSF PFI schools (4 phases)
key_contracts.note: Monthly unitary charges. Phase 1: £1.14M x9 + £1.12M x9 = £20.3M. Locked in, review indexation only.

key_contracts.name: Energy (electricity)
key_contracts.note: Electricity to LCC and schools. DOGE weak competition flag (awarded 1 day after deadline).

key_contracts.name: Energy (gas)
key_contracts.note: Natural gas. DOGE weak competition flag (same).

key_contracts.name: SEND school places expansion
key_contracts.note: 2 new special schools (Autism 70 places, SEMH 70 places), 3 expansions (+100 places), 5 mainstream SEND units, 1 post-19 college.

key_contracts.name: Agency EP supply
key_contracts.note: 110 agency EPs generating 2,400 EHCP advice pieces/year.

savings_levers.description: 50 new Ford minibuses deployed (208 total fleet). Passenger occupancy up 3→5. Cost per passenger down 30% (£11K→£8K). Transport Assistant Grants increased 488→750 saving ~£2M/yr. 200+ parents supporting single-occupancy journeys avoiding £1.8M. Personal Travel Budgets for 400+ families.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Education Act 1996 s508B: must provide transport to eligible children. Statutory distances: 2 miles (under 8), 3 miles (8-16).

data_points: Passenger occupancy up 3→5, cost per passenger down 30%
5,200 SEND children + 900 vulnerable adults transported daily
evidence.benchmark: AI route optimisation in leading councils (Surrey, Kent) achieves 15-25% cost reduction. Lancashire already achieving 30% per-passenger reduction
evidence.calculation: Current trajectory: £61M + £17.7M = £78.7M by 2026/27. If reforms hold growth to £5M instead of £17.7M = £12.7M avoided. Conservative £5-10M accounting for demand growth
evidence.kpi_link: DfE Improvement Notice requires SEND reform plan. More children in mainstream (closer to home) = less transport needed. Each child maintained mainstream vs special saves transport + placement cost

implementation_steps.step: Deploy AI route optimisation across all 12 districts
Expand Personal Travel Budgets to target 800 families (from 400)
Increase Transport Assistant Grants to 1,000 (from 750)
Review all single-occupancy taxi routes: combine or convert to minibus
Negotiate taxi framework with fixed-price contracts (rural areas pricing power)

evidence.political_framing: We've already cut the cost per passenger by 30% with new minibuses. AI route planning and personal travel budgets give families choices while saving millions. This is reform in action.

savings_levers.description: Lancashire 48.4% in special schools (national 38.6%). Every child maintained in mainstream vs special school saves £25-40K/yr in placement + transport. £7.5M recurrent investment into SEND staffing + £37M into places. AI assisting EHCP compliance checking.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Children and Families Act 2014: parental preference for special school must be considered

data_points: Independent sector: 5.1% at avg £57K/pupil vs mainstream £9.1K
£57M capital for 2 new special schools + expansions + 5 mainstream SEND units
evidence.benchmark: National average 38.6% in special schools. Top-performing SEND areas achieve 30-35%. Lancashire at 48.4% is significantly above
evidence.calculation: Reducing special school % from 48.4% to 42% = ~780 children in mainstream instead. At £25-40K/yr saving per child: 780 × £32.5K = £25.4M gross saving. Net of mainstream support costs (~£15K each): £13.7M. Conservative £3-8M in year 1-3 as transition is gradual
evidence.kpi_link: DfE Improvement Notice specifically targets inclusion. Every child successfully included in mainstream = better DfE outcome AND less transport + placement cost

implementation_steps.step: Open 5 mainstream SEND units (already funded from £57M capital)
Deploy Inclusion Service (rebranded from SEND Service) to support mainstream schools
AI-assisted EHCP timeliness: clear backlog by April 2026
Invest in mainstream school SEND training (SENCo capacity)
Early intervention at nursery/reception to prevent escalation to EHCP

evidence.political_framing: Lancashire sends more children to special schools than the national average. Better support in mainstream schools keeps children in their communities, closer to friends and family, and saves £25,000-£40,000 per child per year.

savings_levers.description: Review discretionary transport (post-16, denominational). Route optimisation for statutory transport. Renegotiate taxi contracts.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Statutory distances. Discretionary policies can be changed with consultation.

data_points: 11% increase in transported pupils since Sep 2024
Taxi firms have pricing power in rural areas
Discretionary transport includes post-16, denominational, extended entitlement
evidence.benchmark: Statutory distances: 2 miles (under 8), 3 miles (8-16). Discretionary policies vary, some councils have reduced/removed
evidence.calculation: Discretionary transport estimated 15-20% of total = £9-12M. Policy review could save 10-25% of discretionary = £1-3M. Route optimisation additional
evidence.kpi_link: Not inspection-linked. Political risk from removal of discretionary transport. Must consult and equality impact assess

implementation_steps.step: Map all discretionary transport policies and costs (post-16, denominational, extended)
Benchmark against comparable county councils
Public consultation on proposed changes (statutory 6-week minimum)
Implement revised policy for September start

evidence.political_framing: We must protect statutory transport for every eligible child. But discretionary transport policies need reviewing, some are generous compared to other councils.

savings_levers.description: Npower £110.8M and TotalEnergies £60.0M both flagged for weak competition (awarded 1 day after deadline). Combined £170.8M in energy. Review framework terms and market test.

data_points: Both flagged DOGE weak competition (awarded 1 day after deadline)
LASER or CCS framework likely but terms not publicly visible
evidence.benchmark: Energy procurement best practice: 3-5% saving achievable through framework review, TPI market test, or direct energy purchase. Some councils save 8-12% through energy bureau model
evidence.calculation: £170.8M × 3% = £5.1M. Conservative £2-5M depending on framework lock-in terms. Even 1% improvement = £1.7M
evidence.kpi_link: DOGE weak competition flag evidences lack of competitive tension. This is procurement reform. Contracts Finder publication would enable scrutiny

implementation_steps.step: Review existing energy framework terms (LASER/CCS/other) and identify lock-in periods
Commission independent Third Party Intermediary (TPI) market test
If framework allows, conduct mini-competition between at least 3 suppliers
Consider energy bureau model for direct market purchase (higher saving, higher risk)
Publish contracts on Contracts Finder (currently invisible)

evidence.political_framing: £170 million on energy with no competitive tension. Both contracts flagged as weak competition. We owe it to taxpayers to market-test these and get the best deal.

savings_levers.description: 63 libraries. Community-managed model for low-use branches, digital-first strategy, co-location with other services. No successor to 2022-2025 Libraries Strategy. Requires public consultation.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Public Libraries and Museums Act 1964: must consult and maintain 'comprehensive' service. JR risk (Brent 2011).

data_points: No successor to 2022-2025 Libraries Strategy published
Library of Sanctuary withdrawn by Reform
evidence.benchmark: Suffolk community libraries model: 30% cost reduction. Lincolnshire: 15 community-run of 51. JR risk: Brent 2011 quashed closure, must prove comprehensive service
evidence.calculation: Library service estimated £8-12M/yr. Community transfer of 20 low-use branches (30% of network) could save £2-4M. Requires investment in volunteer coordination (~£500K)
evidence.kpi_link: Not inspection-linked but politically sensitive. Library closures generate significant public opposition. Community transfer is the safest model

implementation_steps.step: Develop new Libraries Strategy (successor to expired 2022-2025)
Identify 20 lowest-footfall branches for community transfer assessment
Public consultation on service model (statutory requirement)
Establish community partnerships and volunteer training
Transfer first 10 branches to community management

evidence.political_framing: We're not closing libraries, we're empowering communities to run them. Community-managed libraries in Suffolk and Lincolnshire have thrived. We'll keep every branch open.

savings_levers.description: LCC provides traded services (HR, finance, payroll, IT, governor support, cleaning, catering) to schools on a buy-back basis. Current penetration is approximately 40% of eligible schools. Growing to 65% through competitive pricing and better service generates £1-3M additional income.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Schools budget regulations, trading powers under LGA 2003 s95

data_points: ~580 maintained schools and academies in Lancashire
Average traded service income per school £8-15K
Key competitors: academy trust central services, private providers
evidence.benchmark: Hampshire CC achieves 70% buy-back rate generating £12M income. Essex CC at 55% generating £8M.
evidence.calculation: 580 schools. 40% currently buying = 232 schools. Target 65% = 377 schools. Additional 145 schools x £10K average = £1.45M. Plus upselling existing schools: £1-3M total.
evidence.kpi_link: Schools satisfaction survey, buy-back retention rate, income targets

implementation_steps.step: Survey all non-buying schools to understand barriers and competitor pricing
Redesign service packages with competitive pricing and SLA guarantees
Active sales campaign targeting academies and free schools
Quarterly review of retention and penetration rates

evidence.political_framing: Lancashire's school support services are excellent. More schools should be buying them. We are competing on quality and price, not relying on loyalty.

savings_levers.lever: Adult learning fees and corporate training income
savings_levers.description: LCC Adult Learning service delivers non-statutory courses alongside ESFA-funded provision. Increasing fees for leisure courses to full cost recovery and developing corporate training packages for Lancashire businesses generates additional income.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: ESFA funding rules (subsidised provision), LGA 2003 trading powers

data_points: Adult Learning budget approximately £15M (mostly ESFA grant)
Lancashire has 45,000+ businesses as potential corporate clients
evidence.benchmark: Devon CC generates £2M from traded adult learning. Staffordshire CC £1.5M. Key: separate fee-paying from grant-funded provision clearly.
evidence.calculation: Fees uplift on 2,000 leisure learners at £50 average increase = £100K. Corporate training: 50 contracts at £5K average = £250K. Plus room hire and venue income: £0.3-1M total.
evidence.kpi_link: ESFA audit, Ofsted adult learning inspection, income per learner

implementation_steps.step: Separate fee-paying and grant-funded provision in accounting
Increase leisure course fees to full cost recovery
Develop corporate training offer (leadership, digital skills, compliance)
Market to Lancashire LEP businesses and Chamber of Commerce members

evidence.political_framing: Our adult learning team has real expertise. Lancashire businesses should be buying it instead of paying consultants from London.

savings_levers.description: LCC has Building Schools for the Future PFI contracts with significant facilities management (FM) components. FM pricing in PFI deals is typically 15-25% above market rates. Active contract management, benchmarking FM costs, and negotiating price reductions on lifecycle items saves £0.5-2M.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: PFI contract terms (change protocol), benchmarking and market testing clauses, Treasury PF2 guidance

data_points: BSF PFI contracts in place for multiple schools
FM component typically 30-40% of unitary charge
Lifecycle costs subject to 5-yearly benchmarking review
evidence.benchmark: Sheffield CC saved £1.8M from PFI FM benchmarking. National Audit Office found 15-25% over-pricing in PFI FM contracts.
evidence.calculation: If LCC PFI annual unitary charge is £20M, FM component = £7M. 10-15% reduction from benchmarking = £0.7-1M. Plus lifecycle cost challenge: £0.5-2M total.
evidence.kpi_link: PFI contract performance indicators, school condition survey

implementation_steps.step: Engage PFI contract management specialist (or in-house if capable)
Benchmark FM costs against market rates using BCIS data
Submit benchmarking/market testing notice under contract provisions
Negotiate revised pricing or trigger market testing clause

evidence.political_framing: PFI contracts were signed years ago at inflated prices. We are using every clause available to bring those costs down to what the market actually charges today.

savings_levers.lever: DSG recovery: out-county SEND placement reduction
savings_levers.description: LCC places children in expensive out-county independent special schools at £40-80K per placement. Investing in local SEND provision (satellite units, resource bases, specialist hubs) to bring 50 children back in-county saves £40K per child in placement costs plus transport.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Children and Families Act 2014, SEND Code of Practice, parental preference rights, First-tier Tribunal appeals

data_points: Estimated 200+ children in out-county independent placements
evidence.benchmark: Cambridgeshire brought 45 children back in-county saving £1.8M in year 1. Wiltshire invested £3M in local provision saving £5M in placements.
evidence.calculation: 50 children x (£60K out-county - £20K in-county) = 50 x £40K = £2M. Plus transport savings: £0.5-1M. Total: £1.5-3M in year 2+.
evidence.kpi_link: DSG management plan, DfE Safety Valve/DBV programme, SEND inspection

implementation_steps.step: Map all out-county placements by need type, cost, and transport cost
Identify 50 children whose needs can be met locally with investment
Commission/develop local provision (satellite classes, resource bases)
Transition children back over 12-24 months with parental engagement

evidence.political_framing: Lancashire children should be educated in Lancashire. We are investing in local special provision so children can stay near their families instead of being sent 100 miles away at enormous cost.

dsg_write_off.condition: Must submit local SEND reform plan to DfE

family_safeguarding_model.evidence_base: Hertfordshire: 46% reduction in children in care

cross_portfolio_dependencies.dependency: Energy contracts (£170.8M combined) need central procurement review. BSF PFI indexation review.
SEND transport uses Ford minibuses from highways fleet procurement. Route optimisation shared.
NHS ICB commitment of £1.8M for neurodivergent pathway. Joint SEND governance required by DfE.

portfolios.title: Economic Development & Growth
directors: Simon Lawrence, Director of Growth and Property

scrutiny_committee.name: Environment, Economic Growth and Transport Scrutiny Committee

statutory_duties.summary: Power to promote economic wellbeing (now general power of competence)
statutory_duties.risk: Discretionary, can shape as needed

key_services: Preston City Deal (£434M programme, PWD complete at £207M, doubled from £104M)
Skelmersdale town centre regeneration (up to £150M public/private investment using public land)
known_pressures: Lancashire productivity gap vs national average
Town centre decline in East Lancashire
Post-levelling-up fund uncertainty
LCCA established Feb 2025: £20M capital + £40.8M adult skills. BSIP flows through CCA from 2025/26.
Preston City Deal overruns: PWD doubled from £104M to £207M. Cottam Parkway ~£27M short. A582 dualling uncertain.
Mike Jensen (Director of Investment) no longer in Jan 2026 structure, capacity gap
Low contract visibility: only 0.6% (£140K) on Contracts Finder

demand_pressures.detail: LCCA established Feb 2025. Brings £20M capital and £40.8M adult skills funding but requires governance overhead and joint working.
£434M programme with significant overruns. PWD doubled to £207M. Cottam Parkway gap ~£27M. A582 dualling uncertain. These are sunk commitments.
£150M public/private programme depends on publicly owned land. Long-term commitment with uncertain returns.

key_contracts.name: Preston City Deal
key_contracts.provider: Various contractors (Walter Carefoot £4.6M, John Turner Construction £4.3M)
key_contracts.note: PWD complete at £207M. Cottam Parkway and A582 dualling outstanding.

savings_levers.description: Review all economic development programmes for outcomes vs cost. Discontinue low-impact schemes. Focus investment on highest-ROI areas.

data_points: Net expenditure £23.7M on cultural and economic development services
Preston City Deal overruns: PWD doubled from £104M to £207M
Lancashire productivity gap vs national average persists
Mike Jensen (Director of Investment) no longer in Jan 2026 structure, capacity gap
evidence.benchmark: Comparator county councils (Kent, Essex, Hampshire) spend £15-20M net on economic development with dedicated ROI tracking. LCC at £23.7M with no published outcomes framework
evidence.calculation: £23.7M net spend. If bottom 10% of programmes by ROI discontinued = £2.4M. Conservative £1-2M after transition costs and political commitments
evidence.kpi_link: No external inspection regime for economic development. Internal KPIs should track GVA uplift, jobs created/safeguarded, private investment leveraged per £1 public spend

implementation_steps.step: Commission independent review of all economic development programmes (ROI per £1 spent)
Rank programmes by outcomes achieved vs cost. Publish results
Discontinue bottom-quartile programmes, redirect funding to high-ROI areas
Establish quarterly outcome reporting for retained programmes

evidence.political_framing: We're spending £24 million on economic development. We need to know what we're getting for that money. Every programme must prove its worth or face the axe.

savings_levers.description: Dedicated external funding team to capture UKSPF, CCA investment. Every £1 in matched funding reduces council contribution.

data_points: LCCA established Feb 2025: £20M capital + £40.8M adult skills
Post-levelling-up fund uncertainty creates gap for proactive bidding
Low Contracts Finder visibility (0.6%) suggests poor external engagement
evidence.benchmark: Cornwall Council external funding team captures £50M+/yr from EU successor funds and national programmes. Greater Manchester CA leverages 4:1 private match on public investment
evidence.calculation: Dedicated 3-person team cost ~£200K/yr. If captures £2M additional external funding, net benefit £1.8M. At 50% match rate: council contribution avoided = £0.5-1M
evidence.kpi_link: CCA governance requirements mean Lancashire must demonstrate funding absorption capacity. Dedicated team ensures compliance and maximises capture

implementation_steps.step: Establish 3-person external funding unit (grant writer, programme manager, data analyst)
Map all available external funding streams (UKSPF, Homes England, DLUHC, CCA)
Submit applications to top 10 funding opportunities
Establish match-funding protocol: council only invests where external leverage available

evidence.political_framing: For every pound we invest in a dedicated funding team, we can capture ten pounds of external investment. Lancashire has been leaving money on the table. We're going to change that.

savings_levers.description: £20M CCA capital + £40.8M adult skills. Ensure Lancashire captures maximum share. Align council programmes with CCA priorities.

data_points: CCA governance still being established, early engagement critical
evidence.benchmark: West Midlands CA has leveraged initial government investment 6:1 through private sector engagement. Greater Manchester CA adult skills budget captures 85%+ of allocation
evidence.calculation: £60.8M total CCA funding (capital + skills). If Lancashire county captures 70% vs baseline 60% = additional 10% = £6.1M. Council benefit from reduced own-spend: £1-3M
evidence.kpi_link: CCA established under Levelling Up and Regeneration Act 2023. Lancashire CC is largest constituent authority and should be lead beneficiary of skills and capital investment

implementation_steps.step: Map all CCA funding streams and governance committees, identify LCC representatives
Align LCC economic development programme priorities with CCA investment plan
Embed LCC officers in CCA programme management teams
Negotiate preferential treatment for LCC skills providers (adult learning service)

evidence.political_framing: The Combined County Authority brings £60 million in new funding to Lancashire. As the county council, we must ensure that money is spent effectively across all 12 districts, not just the cities.

savings_levers.description: Lancashire operates a business rate pool with district councils. Renegotiating the pooling arrangements to retain a larger share of growth above baseline, or optimising the levy/safety net calculations, increases LCC income from business rate growth.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Local Government Finance Act 1988, business rate retention scheme rules, MHCLG pooling prospectus

data_points: Lancashire business rate pool in operation
Growth above baseline generates pooling dividend
District councils and LCC share growth
evidence.benchmark: Greater Manchester pool generates £15M+ annually. Lancashire pool currently modest. Optimisation through better growth forecasting and reset planning.
evidence.calculation: If pool currently generates £10M shared across 15 authorities. Better negotiation of LCC share (county typically gets 9%): additional £0.5-2M.
evidence.kpi_link: NNDR1/NNDR3 returns, business rate collection monitoring, MHCLG reset schedule

implementation_steps.step: Review current pooling agreement and LCC share of growth
Model alternative pooling arrangements (including leaving pool if net negative)
Negotiate revised terms with district council S151 officers
Implement for next financial year

evidence.political_framing: When Lancashire businesses grow, Lancashire should benefit. We are making sure the county council gets its fair share of business rate growth.

savings_levers.description: Empty commercial properties in Lancashire reduce business rate income and blight town centres. While districts collect rates, LCC benefits from higher business rate base. Working with districts to bring 500 empty properties back into use through enforcement, incentives, and compulsory purchase powers generates additional rate income.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: LGA 1972 s215 (untidy land), Town and Country Planning Act 1990 s226 (CPO), Local Government Finance Act 1988 (empty rate relief)

data_points: Thousands of empty commercial properties across Lancashire
Empty property rate relief costs the pool
Each occupied property generates £5-20K in business rates
evidence.benchmark: Birmingham empty property strategy brought 2,000 properties back into use over 3 years. Smaller-scale: 500 properties is realistic for Lancashire.
evidence.calculation: 500 properties back into use x average £2K additional rate yield to LCC (via pool) = £1M gross. Net of enforcement costs: £0.3-1M.
evidence.kpi_link: Business rate base growth, empty property count, town centre vacancy rates

implementation_steps.step: Map empty commercial properties across all 12 districts
Agree joint enforcement protocol with district councils
Target top 100 long-term empty properties with s215 notices
Review CPO powers for strategic sites

evidence.political_framing: Empty shops and offices blight Lancashire towns. We are working with district councils to get them filled, creating jobs and generating rates income.

savings_levers.lever: Council investment portfolio and JV review
savings_levers.description: LCC holds shareholdings, joint ventures, and investments in various companies and partnerships. A systematic review to identify underperforming investments, missed dividend income, and disposal opportunities generates one-off and recurring income.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: S12 LGA 2003 investment powers, Localism Act 2011 general competence, Companies Act duties

data_points: LCC has various company interests and JVs
Dividend and investment income often unmonitored
Some investments may be dormant or underperforming
evidence.benchmark: Standard S151 officer practice to review investment portfolio annually. Many councils find dormant investments worth £0.5-5M on first systematic review.
evidence.calculation: Portfolio review typically identifies £0.5-2M from dividend recovery, disposal of non-strategic investments, and restructuring of underperformers.
evidence.kpi_link: Annual Statement of Accounts, investment strategy, Audit Committee reporting

implementation_steps.step: Compile register of all LCC company interests, JVs, and financial investments
Assess each for performance, strategic value, and disposal potential
Recover missed dividends and restructure underperformers
Dispose of non-strategic holdings

evidence.political_framing: The council has money tied up in investments that are not delivering returns. We are reviewing every one and selling anything that is not working for Lancashire.

savings_levers.description: Stage 2 of the 2027 Tour de France Grand Depart passes 80 miles through Lancashire on 3 July 2027 (Keswick to Liverpool via Lancaster, Bowland, Clitheroe, Blackburn, Chorley). Independent modelling estimates over GBP 15M in additional economic activity. LCC should capture direct income through: fan zone hosting fees (GBP 200-500K), official merchandise concessions (GBP 100-300K), temporary food/drink trading licences along route (GBP 50-150K), park-and-ride operations (GBP 100-200K), camping/glamping on LCC land (GBP 50-100K), and brokering sponsorship packages that bundle LCC highways assets with the event. Yorkshire 2014 Grand Depart generated GBP 131M for the regional economy. With 3.3M spectators lining the Yorkshire route, Lancashire can expect 500K-1M along its section. Commercial programme should be in procurement by Q3 2026.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Licensing Act 2003 (temporary event notices), Road Traffic Regulation Act 1984 (road closures), Highways Act 1980 s115E (objects on highway), planning (temp structures), Food Safety Act 1990, LGA 2003 s93 charging powers

data_points: 80 miles of TdF route through Lancashire on 3 July 2027
Yorkshire 2014: GBP 131M economic impact, 3.3M spectators over 2 days
Westmorland & Furness projecting significant income from Stage 1
Global TV audience 150M+, massive destination marketing value
Route passes through 8 of 12 Lancashire districts
evidence.benchmark: Yorkshire 2014 Grand Depart: GBP 131M economic impact (Sheffield Hallam University study). Welcome to Yorkshire captured over GBP 2M in direct council income. Cambridge Stage 3 2014: GBP 7.2M local impact from single stage. Cumbria 2027 expecting GBP 10M from Stages 1-2.
evidence.calculation: Fan zones (3-5 locations at GBP 50-100K each): GBP 200-500K. Trading concessions (200 pitches at GBP 500-1K): GBP 100-200K. Park-and-ride (10K cars at GBP 10-15): GBP 100-150K. Camping (500 pitches at GBP 30-50): GBP 15-25K. Sponsorship packages: GBP 500K-2M. Merchandise: GBP 100-300K. Business rate uplift from visitor spend: GBP 500K-1M. Total direct: GBP 2-5M. Wider economy: GBP 15M+.
evidence.kpi_link: Economic impact measurement (Sheffield Hallam methodology), visitor count, business rate yield, tourism KPIs

implementation_steps.step: Establish TdF Commercial Board with cabinet member, ED, and Marketing Lancashire
Appoint commercial programme manager (fixed term, self-funding from income)
Map all commercial opportunities along 80-mile Lancashire route section
Design and price sponsorship packages (route section naming, fan zones, hospitality)
Procure concession partners for fan zones, merchandise, food/drink via competitive tender
Negotiate with ASO (race organiser) on commercial rights and exclusion zones
Launch park-and-ride, camping, accommodation booking platform
Deliver event weekend commercial programme 3 July 2027
Commission independent economic impact study within 3 months of event

evidence.political_framing: The Tour de France brings the world's biggest annual sporting event through Lancashire. We are not just hosting it; we are making it pay for Lancashire. Every pound of commercial income from the Tour goes back into Lancashire services. Yorkshire made GBP 131M from their Grand Depart. Lancashire should beat that.

savings_levers.description: Lancashire's landscapes (Bowland AONB, Ribble Valley, coast) and heritage assets attract film/TV production. LCC should establish a filming office charging location fees for use of council-owned land, highways closures, and facilities. Netflix, BBC, ITV and independent productions regularly film in Lancashire but currently pay minimal fees. A proactive filming office with published rate card, fast-track permissions, and a location portfolio generates GBP 0.3-1M annually. Tour de France 2027 media interest multiplies this.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Highways Act 1980 s142 (temporary road closures), Road Traffic Regulation Act 1984, filming generally does not require planning permission (temporary), LGA 2003 s93 charging, property law (licence to occupy)

data_points: Lancashire landscapes featured in multiple TV productions
Bowland AONB, Pendle, Morecambe Bay, Lancaster Castle attract crews
TdF 2027 massively raises Lancashire's profile for location scouts
Film productions typically pay GBP 1-10K per day for council locations
evidence.benchmark: Surrey CC generates GBP 0.5M from filming fees. Kent CC GBP 0.4M. Bristol City Council GBP 0.8M (largest outside London). Screen Manchester: GBP 100M production spend in region.
evidence.calculation: 50 filming days at GBP 2-5K per day: GBP 100-250K. Highway closure fees for productions: GBP 50-100K. Location fees for heritage sites: GBP 50-200K. Total: GBP 0.3-1M.
evidence.kpi_link: Number of filming days, income per production, economic multiplier from crew spend

implementation_steps.step: Create Lancashire filming office within Economic Development (0.5 FTE initially)
Publish online location portfolio with rate card and booking system
Register with Creative England, BFI, Screen Lancashire location database
Market Lancashire as filming destination leveraging TdF 2027 global exposure

evidence.political_framing: Lancashire is one of the most filmed counties in England but we barely charge for it. Other councils make millions. This is about making our beautiful county work harder financially.

portfolios.title: Highways & Transport
directors: Matt Townsend, Director of Highways and Transport

scrutiny_committee.name: Environment, Economic Growth and Transport Scrutiny Committee

statutory_duties.summary: Duty to maintain highways, non-delegable duty of care
statutory_duties.risk: Failure to maintain = liability for injury claims. Claims: £70K/yr (2023/24). 115,000 defects handled 2024/25 (+52% over 5 years).

statutory_duties.summary: Network management duty: keep traffic moving
statutory_duties.risk: Must have network management plan

statutory_duties.act: New Roads and Street Works Act 1991
statutory_duties.summary: Coordinate utility works, recover reinstatement costs
statutory_duties.risk: Revenue opportunity: s59 recharges to utility companies. Permit scheme since March 2015.

statutory_duties.summary: Lead local flood authority duties
statutory_duties.risk: Padiham scheme: £39M (revised from £18M). Construction resumes Spring 2026, completion Winter 2028.

key_services: Highway maintenance (5,900km road network, managed service model since Jun 2025, 50% cost reduction per m-sq)
Pothole repair and resurfacing (defects down 42%: 61K→35K in 6 months. Repair size expanded 2→6 m-sq.)
Street lighting (170,000+ columns, 152,000 LED converted. Cumulative savings £40M+. 26,000+ columns over 40 years old.)
Winter maintenance (58 gritters, 7 depots, ~1,800 miles priority routes, 22,800 tonnes salt, £5-6M/yr)
Public transport coordination (BSIP £67.1M secured. 21M bus journeys H1 2024/25, +10%.)
Flood risk management (Padiham £39M scheme, NW RFCC protecting 40,000+ homes)
known_pressures: Maintenance backlog: £650M to bring network to good condition (revised from £200M). DfT rating: AMBER.
Road deterioration accelerating: A roads red: 2.1%→3.9% (2022→2025). B/C roads red: 3.7%→6.5%. Weather factor: 125% average rainfall, saturated network 6+ months.
DfT funding: £195.3M baseline + £72.7M incentive over 4 years. Additional £12.3M for 2025/26 taking total to £46.8M.
Ford Motor Co vehicle procurement: 18 contracts, ~£7.05M, 5 category monopolies. Multiple same-day awards (30 May 2024). 9 contracts published 95-132 days late.
Climate change: increased flood and storm damage. 2024/25 winter: 18,096 tonnes salt (up from 12,096), 3,869 actions over 85 days.
Bus service BSIP funding flows through CCA from 2025/26, governance uncertainty
2024/25 transport costs overran by £4.3M (SEND transport up 15%+)

demand_pressures.detail: A roads red condition nearly doubled (2.1%→3.9%) in 3 years. B/C roads red up 76% (3.7%→6.5%). Unclassified roads: 27.6% red (stable but high). £650M backlog.
125% average rainfall in 2023. Network saturated 6+ months from summer 2024. Padiham flood scheme cost inflated from £18M to £39M. More frequent extreme weather events.
208 in-house minibuses. 5,200 SEND children + 900 vulnerable adults daily. Each new EHCP potentially adds transport cost. Budget +£17.7M for 2026/27.

demand_pressures.pressure: Tour de France 2027 highway preparation costs (up to GBP 6M)
demand_pressures.detail: Stage 2 passes 80 miles through Lancashire on 3 July 2027. Road preparation (surface repairs, white lining, barriers, signage, traffic management) estimated at GBP 3-6M. Partially offset by commercial income (GBP 0.5-1.5M) and DfT/DCMS funding bids.

key_contracts.name: Ford fleet vehicles
key_contracts.provider: Ford Motor Co Ltd (3 entity variants)
key_contracts.note: 5 category monopolies (tippers, panel vans, vans, passenger cars, minibuses). 7 contracts awarded same day (30/05/2024). Late publications: 95-132 days.

key_contracts.name: Managed highway service
key_contracts.note: Replaced fragmented multi-contractor model. Cost per m-sq reduced from £200 to under £100. 12-month warranty on all repairs.

key_contracts.name: BSIP bus services
key_contracts.note: £1 Evening Fare (2.6M tickets), £1 Sunday Single (780K tickets). Flows through CCA from 2025/26.

key_contracts.name: Highway materials
key_contracts.note: Daily delivery ticket payments, normal for highways. 2,000-4,000 txns each.

key_contracts.name: Padiham flood scheme
key_contracts.note: Partnership with EA, Burnley BC, LEP, NW RFCC.

savings_levers.description: NRSWA 1991 s59 allows councils to charge for excessive road damage. Permit scheme since March 2015. Many councils under-recover. Establish dedicated enforcement team.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: NRSWA 1991: clear legal basis. Case law supports councils.

data_points: NRSWA 1991 s59 provides clear statutory basis
Utility companies responsible for significant road damage
evidence.benchmark: Kent CC recovers £3M+/yr from utility recharges. Transport for London: £5M+. Many councils under-recover by 50%+
evidence.calculation: Estimated 30% of road defects attributable to utility works. 115,000 × 30% = 34,500 defects. At avg £150 reinstatement cost = £5.2M. Recovery rate of 40-80% = £2-5M
evidence.kpi_link: Road condition declining (A roads red 2.1%→3.9%). Utility recharges fund maintenance AND incentivise better utility reinstatement, improving road condition

implementation_steps.step: Establish dedicated s59 enforcement team (3 inspectors + 1 manager)
Audit all current utility works permits vs actual reinstatement quality
Issue s59 notices for all substandard reinstatements
Automated inspection scheduling linked to permit expiry dates

evidence.political_framing: Utility companies dig up our roads and don't fix them properly. We have the legal power to charge them. Kent recovers £3 million a year. Lancashire should be recovering even more.

savings_levers.description: 6 months in: defects down 42%, cost per m-sq down 50%+, repair size tripled, 12-month warranty. Extend model to more service areas. First UK trial of asphalt recycling machine. AI inspections piloted.

data_points: First UK trial of asphalt recycling machine
evidence.benchmark: No other UK county council has achieved 42% defect reduction in 6 months. Managed service model is genuinely innovative
evidence.calculation: Current £72M highways pot. If managed service approach extends to all maintenance: 20% efficiency on £72M = £14.4M. Conservative £3-6M as not all areas suitable
evidence.kpi_link: DfT AMBER rating dependent on road condition. Managed service improves condition (fewer recurring defects) while cutting cost. Win-win for DfT incentive funding

implementation_steps.step: Evaluate Phase 1 results and publish 6-month report
Extend managed service to bridge maintenance (1,800+ structures)
Scale AI inspection from pilot to full network
Deploy asphalt recycling machine at scale (first UK trial already proven)
Benchmark against private sector road maintenance costs

evidence.political_framing: In just six months, our new highway model has cut defects by 42% and halved the cost. We're trialling the first asphalt recycling machine in the UK. This is what reform looks like.

savings_levers.description: 152K of 170K+ columns converted to LED. Cumulative savings £40M+. Part-night dimming in low-risk areas. 26,000+ columns over 40 years old need replacement programme.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Must maintain lighting on classified roads

data_points: Part-night dimming not yet deployed at scale
evidence.benchmark: Essex CC: part-night dimming saved £1.5M/yr. Hampshire: 50% dimming midnight-5am. Buckinghamshire: full switch-off in rural areas
evidence.calculation: 18,000 remaining non-LED columns. Conversion cost ~£500 each = £9M one-off (funded from energy savings within 3 years). Part-night dimming on 50,000 rural/low-risk columns: 5 hours × 365 × £0.015/hr = £1.4M/yr
evidence.kpi_link: Energy costs feed into £170.8M energy contract. Dimming reduces consumption = smaller contract needed at renewal. Also reduces carbon emissions (climate targets)

implementation_steps.step: Complete LED conversion for remaining 18,000 columns
Install CMS (Central Management System) for remote dimming control
Implement part-night dimming on 50,000 rural/low-crime columns (midnight-5am)
26,000 column replacement programme (safety priority), phase over 5 years

evidence.political_framing: LED conversion has already saved £40 million. Part-night dimming in safe rural areas, not on main roads or in towns, can save another £1-2 million a year.

savings_levers.description: Permit fees for street works, advertising on highway assets, parking charges. Review fee levels against statutory maximums.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Fees must not exceed cost of service (except where statute permits)

data_points: 5,900km road network with significant utility works
DfT incentive funding tied to efficient management
Advertising on bridge abutments, roundabouts generates £100K-500K in other councils
evidence.benchmark: Hampshire CC: £2.1M from highway-related fees. Hertfordshire: £1.5M parking + £800K permit fees. Most county councils under-recover against statutory maximums
evidence.calculation: Permit fee review: if 10% below statutory max on 10,000 permits/yr = £100K+. Parking charges review: £200-500K. Advertising: £100-300K. Combined: £1-3M
evidence.kpi_link: DfT incentive funding (£72.7M over 4 years) rewards councils that demonstrate efficient highway management. Income generation demonstrates commercial capability

implementation_steps.step: Review all permit fees against statutory maxima and identify under-recovery
Launch highway advertising programme (roundabouts, bridge abutments)
Review parking charges across all LCC-managed car parks
Establish commercialisation target for highways service

evidence.political_framing: We have legal powers to charge for highway services that we're not fully using. Other councils raise millions. Lancashire should do the same. It funds road repairs.

savings_levers.description: 5 category monopolies, all Ford. Multiple same-day awards. Market test alternative suppliers through TPPL framework. 9 late publications (95-132 days), process fix needed.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Must follow TPPL framework rules

data_points: 5 category monopolies (tippers, panel vans, vans, passenger cars, minibuses)
9 contracts published 95-132 days late on Contracts Finder
3 entity variants used (Ford Motor Company Ltd, Ford Motor Co Ltd, Ford Motor Co)
evidence.benchmark: Best practice: minimum 3 suppliers per vehicle category. DfT fleet benchmarking shows 10-15% savings achievable through competitive tendering across manufacturers
evidence.calculation: £7.05M over 18 contracts at avg £392K. If competitive tendering achieves 10% = £705K. With additional competition from Vauxhall/VW/Toyota: £0.5-1M
evidence.kpi_link: DOGE weak competition and late publication flags. Procurement Act 2023 tightens requirements: must demonstrate value for money and open competition

implementation_steps.step: Audit all Ford contracts and confirm TPPL framework compliance and pricing
Issue competitive tenders for next vehicle replacement cycle (multi-manufacturer)
Fix late publication process: automated Contracts Finder upload
Review same-day award patterns with procurement team

evidence.political_framing: Eighteen contracts worth £7 million, all going to Ford, seven awarded on the same day, nine published months late. That's not competitive procurement, it's a rubber stamp.

savings_levers.lever: Fees and charges review to full cost recovery
savings_levers.description: LCC charges fees for skip licences, road closure permits, streetworks (NRSWA permits), vehicle crossings, and highway searches. Many have not been increased for years and do not reflect full cost recovery. Reviewing every fee line against actual cost of service delivery and inflationary increases generates additional income.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Highways Act 1980 s115/171, NRSWA 1991 s53, TMA 2004 permit fees, full cost recovery principle

data_points: Current fee schedule not updated annually for several categories
Full cost recovery principle applies to most highway fees
evidence.benchmark: Surrey CC increased highways fee income by 18% through systematic review in 2023. Kent CC generates £3.2M from permit scheme.
evidence.calculation: Current highways fee income estimated £5-8M. If 10-20% undercharged: £0.5-1.5M additional.
evidence.kpi_link: Income monitoring, fee benchmarking against CIPFA comparators

implementation_steps.step: List all highways fees and date of last increase
Calculate full cost of service delivery for each fee category
Set revised fee schedule effective from next financial quarter
Implement annual inflationary review mechanism

evidence.political_framing: Utility companies and developers should pay the full cost of the disruption they cause to Lancashire roads. The taxpayer should not be subsidising their profits.

savings_levers.description: Developers are required to fund highway improvements associated with their developments under s278 (works on existing highways) and s38 (adoption of new roads). A proactive approach to identifying and pursuing contributions, rather than waiting for developers to offer, increases income.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Highways Act 1980 s278/s38, NPPF, CIL/s106 framework

data_points: Major developments across Lancashire (Preston, Lancaster, South Ribble)
s278 agreements typically £50-500K per major development
evidence.benchmark: Hampshire CC employs 3 dedicated s278 officers generating £5M annually. Active pursuit generates 2-3x passive income.
evidence.calculation: 20 major developments per year x average £100K contribution = £2M. Plus s38 adoption fees: £0.5-1M. Total: £1-3M.
evidence.kpi_link: Planning decisions, highway improvement programme, developer contribution register

implementation_steps.step: Map all major planning applications with highway impact across 12 districts
Assign dedicated s278 officer to pursue contributions proactively
Standardise contribution calculation methodology (per-trip cost model)
Quarterly reconciliation of contributions received vs developments approved

evidence.political_framing: Developers profit from Lancashire. They should pay for the roads their developments use. We are making sure they do.

savings_levers.description: LCC operates 650+ vehicles. Replacing 200 diesel vehicles with electric equivalents over 2 years saves £2K per vehicle per year in fuel and maintenance. Capital cost offset by Workplace Charging Scheme grant and vehicle replacement budget.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Clean Air Act obligations, fleet operator licensing, workplace charging regulations

evidence.benchmark: Nottingham CC fleet electrification saved £0.8M on 300 vehicles. Government Workplace Charging Scheme provides up to £350 per socket.
evidence.calculation: 200 vehicles x (£3K diesel fuel + £1.5K maintenance - £1K electric fuel - £0.5K e-maintenance) = 200 x £3K = £600K. Plus reduced carbon tax and fleet insurance: £0.3-1M.
evidence.kpi_link: Carbon reduction target, fleet utilisation, fuel spend monitoring

implementation_steps.step: Identify 200 vehicles eligible for electric replacement (urban routes, depot-based)
Procure electric replacements via Crown Commercial Service framework
Install depot charging infrastructure (Workplace Charging Scheme grant)
Phase delivery: 100 vehicles year 1, 100 vehicles year 2

evidence.political_framing: Electric vehicles cost less to run. Switching 200 council vehicles saves fuel money and shows Lancashire is serious about cleaner air.

savings_levers.description: LCC controls thousands of roundabouts, bus shelters, lamp columns, and gantries. Roundabout sponsorship, digital advertising on bus shelters, and naming rights for highway improvements all generate income. Most county councils now operate advertising concessions.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Highways Act 1980 s115, Town and Country Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations 2007, Road Traffic Regulation Act 1984

data_points: Digital advertising technology now available for highway furniture
evidence.benchmark: Kent CC generates £0.6M from roundabout sponsorship. TfL generates significant income from advertising. County-scale schemes typically £0.2-1M.
evidence.calculation: 100 roundabout sponsorships at £1-3K/yr = £100-300K. Bus shelter concession: £100-300K. Other (lamp column banners, gantry advertising): £0.2-0.8M total.
evidence.kpi_link: Income generation targets, planning compliance monitoring

implementation_steps.step: Audit all suitable highway assets for advertising potential
Procure advertising concession partner via competitive tender
Launch roundabout sponsorship scheme across all 12 districts
Expand to digital bus shelter and lamp column advertising

evidence.political_framing: Businesses want to advertise on Lancashire's roundabouts and bus shelters. That income goes straight to fixing roads. Everyone benefits.

savings_levers.lever: Tour de France 2027 highways commercial income
savings_levers.description: The 2027 Tour de France Stage 2 will pass 80 miles through Lancashire on LCC-controlled highways. Road preparation costs are estimated at up to GBP 6M (surface repairs, white lining, signage, barrier installation, temporary traffic management). LCC should offset this through highway advertising, route sponsorship, and temporary structure licences. As highway authority, LCC controls thousands of roundabouts, lamp columns, bus shelters along the route and has sole power to grant TROs. The net cost is GBP 4.5-5.5M after commercial income of GBP 0.5-1.5M. However, this is offset by the wider economic benefit (estimated GBP 15M+) and DfT/DCMS event funding bids. Yorkshire 2014 Grand Depart: Leeds alone generated GBP 400K from highway advertising.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Highways Act 1980 s115E (placing objects on highway), s142 (temp closures), Town and Country Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations 2007, Road Traffic Regulation Act 1984 (TROs), NRSWA 1991 (coordinating with utilities pre-event)

data_points: LCC controls 80 miles of TdF route as highway authority
Road preparation cost estimated up to GBP 6M (surfaces, lining, barriers, TM, staff)
Thousands of roundabouts, lamp columns, bus shelters along route available for advertising
Event requires extensive TROs which LCC has sole power to grant
Highway furniture advertising premium during global event
DfT/DCMS event infrastructure funding bids available to offset costs
evidence.benchmark: Yorkshire 2014: Leeds City Council generated GBP 400K from highway advertising alone during TdF. TfL generates GBP 160M/yr from highway advertising assets.
evidence.calculation: COSTS: Road surface repairs and preparation (GBP 3-4M), white lining and signage (GBP 0.5-1M), barrier and street furniture (GBP 0.5-0.8M), temporary traffic management (GBP 0.3-0.5M), staff/overtime (GBP 0.2-0.3M). Total estimated cost: up to GBP 6M. INCOME: Route banner advertising (50 sites at GBP 3-5K): GBP 150-250K. Premium roundabout sponsorship (30 roundabouts at GBP 3-5K, TdF premium): GBP 90-150K. Lamp column banners in route towns: GBP 50-100K. Highway land hire for fan zones/hospitality: GBP 100-300K. Temporary structure licences: GBP 50-100K. Total income: GBP 0.5-1.5M. NET COST: GBP 4.5-5.5M before external funding bids (DfT/DCMS/Sport England).
evidence.kpi_link: Income per highway asset, advertising compliance, DfT incentive funding alignment

implementation_steps.step: Audit all highway assets along TdF route for advertising potential
Create TdF premium sponsorship packages bundling roundabouts, lampcolumns, banners
Procure advertising concession partner for route with TdF uplift clause
Issue TROs for commercial fan zone areas on highway land
Deliver highway advertising programme for event weekend

evidence.political_framing: The Tour de France needs Lancashire's roads in top condition. We are investing up to GBP 6M to prepare our highways, and we are making every penny work harder by generating commercial income from the route. The roads will be better for Lancashire residents long after the race has passed.

portfolios.title: Rural Affairs, Environment & Communities
directors: Jonathan Edwards, Director of Environment and Planning
Clare Coupe, Director of Communities and Engagement

scrutiny_committee.name: Environment, Economic Growth and Transport Scrutiny Committee

statutory_duties.summary: Waste disposal authority: must arrange disposal of controlled waste
statutory_duties.risk: Cannot stop waste disposal. Lancashire Renewables (£71M) is arms-length wholly-owned company. Suez (£46.2M) is 60.7% of category.

statutory_duties.summary: Biodiversity duty, waste strategy, food waste collection from Apr 2026
statutory_duties.risk: New duties. Lancashire Renewables expanding to food waste processing.

statutory_duties.summary: Emergency planning and resilience
statutory_duties.risk: Category 1 responder: must maintain emergency plans

statutory_duties.summary: Trading standards enforcement
statutory_duties.risk: ~50-70 staff, £2.95M budget. Can reduce but not eliminate.

key_services: Waste disposal (Lancashire Renewables, wholly owned, 2 MBT plants, 500K tonnes/yr capacity, 95% diversion from landfill, ~250 employees, 35 HGVs)
Waste acceptance and recovery (Suez: North Lancashire £12.4M, FCC: South £13.0M + East £10.3M. Contracts to 2030.)
Food waste processing (from April 2026, Lancashire Renewables expanding)
Trading standards and consumer protection (~600 bogus caller reports + ~600 scam reports/year)
known_pressures: Lancashire Renewables: £71.2M (20 months), wholly-owned, ex-PFI (terminated 2014, just 3 years into 25-year £2B PFI). 2 MBT plants cost £250M to build. 3.80 MW electricity generation.
Suez: 60.7% of category (HHI 4,141, HIGH). £46.2M (20 months). Whinney Hill landfill extended to Mar 2026.
EfW procurement paused. Suez, Veolia, Biffa, Miller Turner expressed interest but council reviewing waste strategy (previous strategy expired 2020). No replacement strategy published.
Envirofuel: 2-year contract for East Lancashire waste (100K+ tonnes/year). Converts to fuel for cement works.
Environment Act 2021: new biodiversity and food waste duties from Apr 2026
Climate action commitments
Trading standards: Rogue roofer jailed 1 year (Jan 2026). £1M confiscation order (Jeffreys, Colne). £1.65M illicit tobacco/vapes seized (Oswaldtwistle).
0.1% contract coverage on Contracts Finder (£99.5K of £94.3M outturn), massive transparency gap

demand_pressures.detail: Lancashire Renewables (wholly-owned) + Suez (60.7%) = £117M duopoly over 20 months. PFI terminated but plant costs are sunk. Inflation-indexed. EfW strategy paused.
From April 2026, source-separated food waste processing required. Lancashire Renewables expanding but additional cost.
Previous waste strategy expired 2020. No successor published. EfW procurement paused. Suez applied for AD plant in Blackburn as alternative, 'unclear whether EfW facility will be developed'.

key_contracts.name: Lancashire Renewables Ltd
key_contracts.note: 2 MBT plants (Farington, Thornton). 500K tonnes capacity. 95% landfill diversion. £250M build cost. 3.80 MW generation.

key_contracts.name: Non-recyclable waste (3 lots)
key_contracts.note: Lot structure provides some competition but Suez dominant overall.

key_contracts.name: Envirofuel East Lancashire
key_contracts.note: 100K+ tonnes/year from East Lancashire. Converts waste to fuel for cement works.

key_contracts.name: Lancashire Environmental Fund
key_contracts.provider: SUEZ + LCC + Lancashire Wildlife Trust + Community Futures
key_contracts.note: Partnership for environmental improvements.

savings_levers.description: Lancashire Renewables £71M + Suez £46M = £117M. Even 5% saving = £5.8M. Review indexation terms, performance deductions, recycling credits. EfW strategy needed: current vacuum is costing money. Suez non-recyclable waste contracts expire 2030.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: PFI legacy terms may limit scope. Legal review of terms needed.

data_points: Waste strategy expired 2020, no successor published
evidence.benchmark: National landfill rate 5.6%. Lancashire 33.8% = 6x national average. EfW councils achieve <5% landfill. Waste strategy vacuum is the primary cause
evidence.calculation: £117M combined. Indexation review + performance deductions could yield 3-5% = £3.5-5.9M. EfW strategy decision could transform long-term trajectory
evidence.kpi_link: Landfill rate 6x national average is both environmental failure and financial cost (landfill tax escalator). EfW would reduce gate fees long-term

implementation_steps.step: Commission independent legal review of Lancashire Renewables and Suez terms
Develop new Waste Strategy (successor to expired 2020 strategy)
Review all indexation clauses and challenge above-CPI increases
Prepare Suez contract exit/retender strategy for 2030 expiry
Make EfW decision: procurement or AD alternative (Suez Blackburn proposal)

evidence.political_framing: Lancashire sends 6 times more waste to landfill than the national average because the previous administration never decided on energy-from-waste. We're paying for their indecision: £117 million in waste contracts with no published strategy.

savings_levers.description: Shift from reactive to intelligence-led enforcement. Digital-first consumer advice. Regional collaboration. Safe Trader scheme income.

evidence.benchmark: Buckinghamshire 'Safe Trader' scheme generates £200K+/yr income. Surrey trading standards hub serves 11 boroughs, 30% cost reduction
evidence.calculation: £2.95M budget. Intelligence-led targeting (80:20 rule) + Safe Trader income (£200K) + digital consumer advice (reduce phone calls 40%) = £0.5-1M efficiency
evidence.kpi_link: National Trading Standards Board tracks prosecution success and consumer detriment prevented. Revenue from Proceeds of Crime Act recoveries funds further enforcement

implementation_steps.step: Implement intelligence-led targeting, focus on highest-harm cases
Launch Safe Trader approved scheme (trader pays for vetting/endorsement)
Migrate consumer advice to digital-first (chatbot, online reporting)
Explore regional hub model with Blackpool/Blackburn unitaries

evidence.political_framing: Our trading standards team jailed a rogue roofer, seized £1.65 million in illicit tobacco, and recovered £1 million from a fraudster. We're making enforcement smarter and generating income from the Safe Trader scheme.

savings_levers.description: Review countryside properties and access points. Community transfer where appropriate.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Countryside and Rights of Way Act 2000: must maintain definitive map

data_points: Multiple countryside sites and access points across Lancashire
Definitive map maintenance is statutory but property management is discretionary
Community groups often willing to take over management of local countryside sites
evidence.benchmark: National Trust, Wildlife Trusts and parish councils manage countryside access across England. Community transfer model proven in Lake District and Peak District
evidence.calculation: Countryside management estimated £1-2M. Community transfer of 5-10 lower-priority sites at avg £50K/yr running cost = £250-500K. Minimal capital outlay
evidence.kpi_link: Biodiversity duty under Environment Act 2021 requires net gain but doesn't require council-managed sites. Community management can improve biodiversity outcomes

implementation_steps.step: Audit all countryside sites: footfall, costs, community interest
Identify 5-10 sites suitable for community transfer
Negotiate management agreements with local groups, parishes, wildlife trusts
Transfer management responsibilities with transition funding

evidence.political_framing: Local communities care about their countryside more than any council officer. We'll empower parish councils and community groups to manage local green spaces while meeting our legal duties.

savings_levers.description: 0.1% contract coverage on Contracts Finder. Publish all waste and environment contracts to enable scrutiny and competition.

data_points: 0.1% contract coverage on Contracts Finder (£99.5K of £94.3M outturn)
Suez HHI 4,141, dominant position enabled by opacity
evidence.benchmark: Transparency Code 2015 requires publication of spending over £500 and contracts over £5K. Most councils achieve 60-80% coverage. Lancashire environment at 0.1% is lowest in the county
evidence.calculation: Zero direct saving but transparency enables: competitive tension (reduces prices 5-15%), public scrutiny (prevents waste), benchmarking (identifies overcharging), political accountability
evidence.kpi_link: Procurement Act 2023 mandates publication. Non-compliance risks legal challenge. Proactive publication demonstrates Reform commitment to accountability

implementation_steps.step: Audit all environment/waste contracts and compile complete register
Publish all contracts over £5K on Contracts Finder (legal requirement)
Establish automated publication workflow for new contracts

evidence.political_framing: Only 0.1% of this portfolio's contracts are publicly visible. £117 million in waste contracts that nobody can scrutinise. We're publishing every contract, because sunlight is the best disinfectant.

savings_levers.lever: Country park and countryside income generation
savings_levers.description: LCC manages country parks, woodlands, and countryside sites. Introducing or increasing car parking charges, event hire fees, camping and glamping concessions, and cafe leases generates income from assets that currently cost money to maintain.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: CROW Act 2000 (right of access), road traffic orders for parking charges, planning permission for new activities

data_points: Multiple country parks and countryside sites
Many have no car parking charges
Event and wedding venue potential underexploited
evidence.benchmark: National Trust charges £5-8 parking at comparable sites. Surrey CC country parks generate £1.2M net income. Lancashire sites currently cost-neutral or loss-making.
evidence.calculation: 10 sites x average £30-60K additional income (parking, events, concessions) = £0.3-0.6M. Plus glamping concession: £0.2-0.8M total.
evidence.kpi_link: Visitor numbers, income per visitor, site maintenance cost vs income

implementation_steps.step: Survey all countryside sites for income potential
Introduce car parking charges at sites without them (traffic regulation order)
Tender concessions for cafes, camping, events at 5 key sites
Monitor income and reinvest in site quality

evidence.political_framing: Our countryside sites are beautiful but they cost money to maintain. Modest parking charges and cafe concessions mean visitors help fund the upkeep. The alternative is closing sites.

savings_levers.description: Waste collection routes are often based on historical patterns rather than optimal routing. GPS tracking and route optimisation software can reduce miles driven, fuel consumed, and overtime worked. Typical savings: 10-15% on fuel and labour.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: EPA 1990 waste collection duties (statutory service), employment law for changed working patterns

data_points: Waste collection across Lancashire (via district contracts or LCC direct)
Routes not reviewed systematically for years
evidence.benchmark: Birmingham CC saved £1.2M from route optimisation. Smaller scale: £0.3-1M for Lancashire county waste operations.
evidence.calculation: If LCC waste-related vehicle costs £4-6M annually. 10% route optimisation saving = £0.4-0.6M. Plus overtime reduction: £0.3-1M total.
evidence.kpi_link: Waste collection KPIs, fuel spend per tonne, missed collection rate

implementation_steps.step: Install GPS tracking on all waste fleet vehicles
Procure route optimisation software and model current vs optimal routes
Implement revised routes with driver consultation
Monitor fuel consumption and overtime weekly

evidence.political_framing: Council waste trucks drive thousands of unnecessary miles every year on outdated routes. GPS and modern software cuts fuel costs and gets the job done faster.

savings_levers.description: LCC owns agricultural land let to tenants. Farm rents have risen significantly but council rents are often years out of date. A systematic rent review against market rates, pursued through RICS-qualified valuers, increases rental income.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: Agricultural Holdings Act 1986, Agricultural Tenancies Act 1995, RICS Red Book valuation standards

data_points: LCC agricultural estate covers significant acreage
Some rents not reviewed for 3-7 years
Agricultural land values and rents have increased 20-40% since 2020
evidence.benchmark: County farm estates typically generate £200-500K additional income after systematic review. Gloucestershire CC achieved £350K uplift.
evidence.calculation: If current agricultural rent roll £1-2M. Market review achieves 15-25% increase = £0.15-0.5M.
evidence.kpi_link: Estate income monitoring, RICS Red Book compliance, agricultural land price index

implementation_steps.step: Commission RICS-qualified agricultural valuer to review all farm tenancies
Serve rent review notices on tenancies where below market
Negotiate revised rents (or refer to arbitration if disputed)

evidence.political_framing: Council-owned farm land should generate a fair rent. Some tenancies have not been reviewed for years. We are bringing rents in line with what the land is worth.

savings_levers.lever: Tour de France 2027 countryside and events income
savings_levers.description: The TdF Stage 2 route passes directly through LCC countryside assets: Bowland AONB, Beacon Fell Country Park, and multiple LCC-managed green spaces. Opportunity: temporary camping/glamping sites on LCC land (500-1,000 pitches at GBP 25-50/night for 2-3 nights), event catering concessions at countryside viewing points, premium hospitality areas at categorised climbs (Jubilee Tower, Rivington), cycling festival in Bowland weekend before race. Post-event: establish annual Lancashire cycling sportive using TdF route, generating ongoing income from entry fees and sponsorship.
savings_levers.legal_constraints: CROW Act 2000 (access land restrictions), Countryside and Rights of Way Act, planning permission for temporary camping (28-day rule), Licensing Act 2003 (alcohol sales), Environmental Protection Act 1990 (noise), LCC land ownership and commons registration

data_points: TdF route passes through Bowland AONB and multiple LCC countryside sites
Accommodation in rural Lancashire already limited, camping demand huge
Post-event sportive could become annual revenue source
evidence.benchmark: Yorkshire Festival 2014 around TdF: GBP 2.8M economic impact from fringe events alone. Sportive entry fees: Etape du Tour charges EUR 150-300 per rider, 15K riders.
evidence.calculation: Camping (750 pitches x 2 nights x GBP 35): GBP 52K. Premium hospitality (200 tickets at GBP 100-250): GBP 20-50K. Catering concessions (20 sites at GBP 2-5K): GBP 40-100K. Cycling festival: GBP 50-150K. Annual sportive post-event (5K riders at GBP 40): GBP 200K ongoing. Total year 1: GBP 0.3-0.8M.
